name: Boumerdes
description: Coastal province east of Algiers with reliable winter Mediterranean swells influenced by seasonal winds, supporting a modest local surf scene.

name: El Tarf
description: Remote eastern coastline near Tunisia, featuring rugged rocky points and seasonal waves shaped by winter mistral winds.

name: Oman
description: Western Algeria’s coast with long sandy beaches and generally gentle surf conditions.

name: Chiundo
description: Southern coastal region known for reef and beach breaks with consistent swells from the South Atlantic. Popular with local surfers, it features less crowded waves and a rugged, natural coastline.

name: Kwanza Sul
description: Mid-coast region near the Kwanza River mouth with a mix of sandy beach breaks and reef breaks. It offers reliable swells and is accessible from nearby towns, making it a growing spot for surf tourism.

name: Luanda
description: The capital city region featuring urban beach breaks along the coast. The waves are more influenced by local wind conditions and tides, offering both beginner and intermediate surfing opportunities.

name: Zaire
description: Northernmost surf region near the Congo border with reef breaks and rocky points. It's relatively remote, with less consistent swell but high-quality waves when conditions align.

name: Southwest Region
description: Volcanic coastline around Limbe with consistent beach and reef breaks; Cameroon's main surf hub.

name: South Region
description: Sandy beaches and river mouths near Kribi and Campo; less consistent but seasonally surfable.

name: Boa Vista
description: Eastern island with long sandy beaches and consistent beach breaks; quieter than Sal.

name: Chã de Alecrim
description: Neighborhood in Mindelo, São Vicente; gateway to local breaks like Salamansa and São Pedro.

name: Praia
description: Capital on Santiago Island with occasional beach breaks near Prainha and Gamboa.

name: Sal
description: Cape Verde’s top tourist island, home to Santa Maria and world-class reef breaks like Ponta Preta.

name: Sao Vicente
description: Island with reef and beach breaks exposed to NE trades; includes Salamansa and São Pedro.

name: Tarrafal
description: Northern surf town on Santiago Island with a mellow beach break and tourist-friendly vibe.

name: Tarrafal de Sao Nicolau
description: West coast town on São Nicolau Island, near reef break Sabi Sabi with clean right-handers.

name: Alexandria
description: Historic city with Egypt’s most active surf scene. Small reefs and beachbreaks light up in winter, with year-round surf schools along the hotel-lined coast.

name: Matruh
description: Marsa Matruh offers cleaner, more powerful winter swell than Alexandria. Agiba provides some shelter, while westward points hold rare but promising lefts when conditions align.

name: Estuaire
description: Covers the surfable coastal stretch around Libreville and Pointe-Denis, including spots like Guegue and Ngombe. Expect steady point and beach breaks, best in spring and summer with SW swells.

name: Ogooue Maritime
description: Encompasses Port-Gentil and nearby beaches (e.g., Cap Lopez, Palplanche). Known for hollow, powerful beach breaks over sand, reliable with SW swells.

name: Mayumba
description: A remote stretch near the Congo border with long, fast lefts breaking over sand. It’s isolated and rarely crowded, catching solid southern swells during the dry season.

name: Central Region
description: A quiet stretch around Winneba and Kormantse where beach breaks come alive when the swell rolls in. Waves aren’t as consistent as the west but the spots stay uncrowded.

name: Greater Accra Region
description: The coastline near Accra sees year-round surf with mellow beach breaks and occasional reefs. Waves tend to be small and clean, especially in the dry season, making this a beginner-friendly zone with easy access from the capital.

name: Western Region
description: This coastline delivers consistent beach and point breaks. Busua features playful peaks, and Cape Three Points offers long, powerful lefts when southern swells arrive.

name: Abidjan
description: Includes beach breaks at Cité Police and Vridi Plage near the city. Waves break over sandy bottoms with consistent small to medium surf influenced by the lagoon outlet.

name: Bas-Sassandra
description: From Sassandra to Grand-Béréby, the coastline has rugged reef breaks and beach breaks exposed to strong southern swells. Sassandra’s reefs are well known for powerful lefts.

name: Comoè
description: Spans the coastline east of Assinie to the Ghana border. Features remote, mostly sandy beach breaks with occasional rocky outcrops, influenced by the Comoé river estuary.

name: Lagunes
description: Covers Grand-Bassam and Assinie with exposed beach breaks like La Passe and sheltered sandbanks near Assinie-Mafia. The coastline is shaped by the interaction of ocean swell and river outflows.

name: Coast Province
description: Surf spots run from Malindi to Diani Beach with beach and reef breaks. The area receives consistent swells mainly during the spring and autumn months.

name: Grand Bassa County
description: Covers the coast around Buchanan. More exposed to open ocean swell with scattered beach breaks and potential at river mouths.

name: Grand Cape Mount County
description: Home to Robertsport, Liberia’s premier surf spot, with long left-hand point breaks peeling along rocky headlands near Lake Piso.

name: Montserrado County
description: Centered around Monrovia, this stretch offers small beach breaks like Fisherman’s Beach, with a growing local surf scene along the city’s coastal suburbs.

name: Androy Region
description: Remote and rugged southern tip near Cap Sainte Marie. Limited access but potential for uncrowded, powerful beach breaks.

name: Anosy Region
description: Southeastern coastline around Fort Dauphin. Consistent exposure to Indian Ocean swell with a mix of beach and reef breaks.

name: Antsepoka
description: Quiet coastal stretch west of Tulear. Picks up southwest swell with mellow reef setups and minimal crowds.

name: Atsimo-Andrefana Region
description: Southwest hub with world-class reef breaks like Flameballs and Anakao. Reliable swell and boat-accessible waves near Tulear.

name: Fampotakely
description: Remote northeastern stretch near Ambohitralanana, where exposed reef setups can catch solid Indian Ocean swell. Little documentation, but signs of promising surf potential.

name: Nouakchott
description: Centered on the city’s old port, where broken wharf pylons help form short, wedgy rights during NW winter swells. Currents and wind-blown sand are constant, but it's uncrowded and exposed.

name: Black River District
description: Mauritius’s southwest corner is the island’s surf hub, with a mix of powerful reef breaks and sheltered corners. Dry, open terrain gives way to consistent swell exposure and some of the longest waves on the island.

name: Pamplemousses District
description: The northwestern coastline sees less swell but offers mellow reef setups when conditions align. Warmer waters and gentler surf make it a more relaxed alternative to the south and west.

name: Rodrigues
description: Remote and far less developed, Rodrigues occasionally sees small surf on exposed reef passes. Conditions are fickle, but the quiet setting and tropical surroundings make it a unique outpost.

name: Savanne District
description: Mauritius’s south coast is rugged and dramatic, with cliffs and open beaches facing directly into southern ocean swell. The surf is raw and exposed, with powerful conditions during peak swell windows.

name: Casablanca-Settat
description: Dense coastline of beach breaks and piers around Casablanca, with regular swell and an active local scene. Less consistent the farther south you go, but some exposed reefs offer more variety.

name: Guelmim-Oued Noun
description: A vast and underexplored region, home to Morocco’s southernmost established surf scene. Around Sidi Ifni and Mirleft, rugged cliffs and remote beaches hide punchy beach breaks and rocky points. Further south, the coastline grows emptier, with potential for discovery but little infrastructure.

name: Marrakesh-Safi
description: Marrakesh lures tourists with its souks and spice-scented alleys, but the coastline hides real treasure. Safi delivers Morocco’s heaviest right-hand barrel, while the surrounding shore offers uncrowded setups for those willing to search.

name: Oriental
description: Remote Mediterranean coastline with rare, wind-dependent waves near Saïdia and Ras Kebdana. Minimal infrastructure, few crowds, and highly inconsistent surf.

name: Rabat-Salé-Kenitra
description: Urban stretch with exposed beach breaks and jetties, particularly around the capital, Rabat. Picks up north-west swells and has a mix of easy-access city spots and heavier sandbar setups in rural pockets.

name: Souss-Massa
description: Once a quiet fishing village, now the poster child of Moroccan surf. Iconic right-hand points like Anchor Point and Killers fire through winter, drawing heavy crowds. The town's packed with surf camps, cafés, and foreign tourists, softening its Berber roots, but it's still the place for a tagine and some epic, but crowded waves.

name: Tangier-Tetouan-Al Hoceima
description: Covering Morocco’s Mediterranean coastline near the Strait of Gibraltar. Surf is inconsistent, with small wind swell and rare Atlantic pulses reaching select sheltered breaks.

name: Tagazhout
description: Once a quiet fishing village, now the poster child of Moroccan surf. Iconic right-hand points like Anchor Point and Killers fire through winter, drawing heavy crowds. The town's packed with surf camps, cafés, and foreign tourists, softening its Berber roots — but it’s still the place for a tagine and some epic, but crowded waves.

name: Essaouira
description: A laid-back region mixing mellow beach breaks with Imsouane’s famously long right-hand point. The old medina and fishing port give the area a strong cultural identity, while the growing surf scene adds a modern edge. Less hectic than Taghazout but still full of character.

name: Gaza Province
description: Mozambique's southernmost coast, home to Ponta do Ouro, a long right-hand point break near the South African border. Often overlooked, this quiet region gets clean lines during southern Indian Ocean swells, drawing road-tripping South Africans.

name: Inhambane Province
description: Mozambique’s surf hub with consistent beach and reef breaks around Tofo, Tofinho, and Barra. Curved coastline faces southeast, pulling in regular swell. Supported by a growing surf scene and traveler-friendly vibe.

name: Maputo Province
description: Mozambique’s southernmost coast, home to Ponta do Ouro — a long right-hand point break near the South African border. Often overlooked, this quiet region gets clean lines during southern Indian Ocean swells, drawing road-tripping South Africans.

name: Erongo
description: Namibia’s surf heartland. Home to Skeleton Bay, a legendary left-hand sand point near Walvis Bay that draws pros chasing endless barrels. Also includes Swakopmund and surrounding beaches that work in solid SW swells.

name: Karas
description: The southernmost stretch of Namibia’s coast, Karas receives strong southern swells and is known for its raw, exposed beaches near Lüderitz. Cold waters and wind-blasted terrain make it ideal for seasoned surfers chasing isolation and power.

name: Kunene
description: Up near the Angolan border, Kunene is remote and mostly uncharted. Swells still reach this part of the Skeleton Coast, and while access is limited, the untouched beaches hold promise for those willing to go off the map.

name: Lagos
description: Centered around Nigeria’s bustling coastal hub, Lagos offers a modest but growing surf scene. Breaks like Tarkwa Bay and Lighthouse Beach provide consistent, beginner-friendly waves, especially during the wet season from May to September.

name: Atlantic
description: Covering the stretch around Cotonou, Benin’s largest city, this region sees mellow beach breaks with potential during south swells. While still under the radar, it offers warm waters and uncrowded conditions.

name: Ouémé
description: Just east of Cotonou, Ouémé features quiet coastal villages and expansive beaches. Swell exposure drops slightly here, but it's still suited for small, clean longboard days when conditions align.

name: Pointe Noire
description: Sheltered between headlands and estuaries, Pointe-Noire is the Republic of Congo’s lone surfable stretch. The area sees mellow Atlantic swells, with beach breaks that occasionally offer shape near river mouths. Surf here is rare, but when conditions align, it’s clean and uncrowded.

name: Bioko South
description: The wild southern coastline of Bioko Island, surprisingly off Cameroon rather than mainland Equatorial Guinea. Volcanic headlands and jungle backed beaches face into south swells, with reef passes and open breaks offering little known but empty surf potential.

name: Saint Anthony of Ureca
description: Remote village on Bioko’s southern tip, one of Africa’s wettest places. Exposed to direct south swell, the rocky points and reef ledges here can deliver powerful, raw waves in complete isolation.

name: Armagnac
description: Southwest facing reef that catches strong Indian Ocean swell. Can deliver hollow, powerful waves but conditions are fickle and best suited to experienced surfers.

name: Domaine d'Anjou
description: West coast reef zone offering smaller, more sheltered waves compared to Réunion’s heavier breaks. Works best on moderate swells, making it a friendlier option when the surf elsewhere is intense.

name: L'Établissement
description: Eastern shoreline around Saint-André. Exposed directly to the trade winds, giving it constant swell but often messy conditions. Surf is usually powerful and challenging, with reef-dominated breaks. Best during lighter winds or rare offshore patterns.

name: La Balance
description: Southern coastal stretch near Saint-Pierre. Open to south and southwest swell, producing strong, consistent waves over reef. Heavy currents and powerful surf are common, making it a high-energy zone.

name: La Ligne Paradis
description: West-facing section near Saint-Louis. Picks up southwest swell but has some shelter from trades, offering more manageable conditions compared to the east. Reefs dominate, with waves ranging from playful to heavy depending on swell size.

name: La Pointe au Sel
description: Southwest-facing area near Saint-Leu. Direct exposure to southern swells makes it one of Réunion’s most consistent zones. Mostly reef, producing long, powerful walls. Currents and shallow sections demand experience.

name: Lotissement Bellevue
description: Situated inland from Le Port on the northwest coast. Faces west-northwest, catching occasional swells that wrap around the island. Conditions are more sheltered here, with smaller, cleaner waves compared to the southern coast.

name: Résidence Champagne
description: Northwest coastal sector near Saint-Paul. Oriented west, with reefs that light up on southwest swells. Offers a mix of playful and challenging waves, depending on tide and swell angle. Often cleaner than the exposed east.

name: Réunion
description: The full island region. Réunion’s coastline combines wind-exposed east and northeast zones with heavy, consistent southern reef surf and somewhat more sheltered western areas. A true Indian Ocean island environment with powerful waves and limited beginner options.

name: Villa Bourbon
description: Southwest-facing coastal pocket south of Saint-Leu. Exposed to the same swell trains as La Pointe au Sel but slightly more sheltered. Still reef-heavy, with hollow, powerful sections that attract experienced surfers.

name: Dakar
description: Peninsula region and the country’s surf hub, home to Ngor Right made famous in the film The Endless Summer. The Almadies peninsula picks up predominantly north to northwest groundswells in the main season and offers a compact mix of reef, point and beach breaks that work on a range of tides.

name: Thiès Region
description: Coastal corridor south of Dakar from Popenguine through Somone, Saly and Mbour. Lined by long sandy beaches with intermittent reef patches, it responds to north and northwest winter swells and sees occasional southwest wraps; waves are generally mellower than Dakar and offer more consistent beach break options for intermediate surfers.

name: Saint Louis
description: Northern sector around the Langue de Barbarie and Saint Louis. Long sandy beaches and river mouth breaks that respond to north and northwest swell pulses. Less surf infrastructure and more variable conditions, but important for north swell days and exploratory beach sessions.

name: Mahé
description: Largest island and the most surf-diverse in the Seychelles, with reef and beach breaks spread around its coastline. The south and southeast coasts offer the most reliable waves during the southeast monsoon from May to September, with spots like Anse Intendance and Grand Anse. The west coast is more sheltered, producing smaller conditions around Beau Vallon. Victoria serves as the main hub, while swell consistency varies by exposure, favouring southern shores.

name: Praslin
description: The second largest island with limited but scenic surf opportunities, mainly on the east and north coasts. Reef breaks like Anse Gaulette work during southeast swells, while beaches such as Anse Lazio can produce occasional waves on larger swell events. Surf here is less consistent than Mahé but typically uncrowded, with the best conditions during the southeast monsoon season.

name: La Digue
description: A compact, laid-back island known for powerful reef breaks and a relaxed, car-free atmosphere. Grand Anse on the southeast coast is the primary surf area, firing during southeast swells when wind and tide align. Surf conditions are highly sensitive, while iconic beaches like Anse Source d’Argent are better known for scenery than waves.

name: Western Area
description: A forested peninsula wrapping around the capital, where steep hills drop directly into the Atlantic and create a dense concentration of surfable beaches. The west- and south-facing coastline is exposed to consistent Atlantic swell, producing rideable waves year-round with cleaner conditions during the dry season from November to April. Access is relatively straightforward, with coastal roads linking the city to long, open beaches backed by lagoons and protected forest. Much of the interior is covered by national parkland, limiting development and keeping large sections of the coastline largely undeveloped.

name: Eastern Cape
description: Eastern Cape province featuring world-famous Jeffreys Bay, home to Supertubes and other legendary right-hand point breaks. The region offers consistent surf year-round with peak conditions during winter when powerful swells arrive from the Southern Ocean. Beyond J-Bay, the coast includes diverse breaks from beach breaks to reef setups, with the Garden Route providing scenic backdrops. The region combines premier surf destinations with dramatic coastline, wildlife viewing opportunities, and access to the Tsitsikamma National Park.

name: KwaZulu-Natal
description: Province known for warm water surfing and vibrant surf culture centered around Durban. The Golden Mile offers accessible beach breaks suitable for all levels, while spots like New Pier and Cave Rock provide more challenging reef breaks. The region receives consistent swells year-round with peak conditions during winter months. Durban serves as the main hub with markets, colonial architecture, and a lively beachfront scene. The province also includes the Drakensberg Mountains and game reserves, offering diverse experiences beyond the coast.

name: Western Cape
description: Province home to Cape Town and some of South Africa's most iconic surf breaks. The region offers diverse conditions from powerful reef breaks like Dungeons to accessible beach breaks along the False Bay and Atlantic coasts. Cape Town provides the urban hub with Table Mountain, vibrant markets, and cultural attractions. The region features dramatic scenery where mountains meet ocean, with the Cape Peninsula offering both sheltered and exposed breaks. Best surf conditions occur during winter when large swells arrive from the Southern Ocean.

name: Prince
description: Smaller of the two main islands with limited documented surf breaks. The island features pristine beaches and tropical rainforest, with occasional surfable conditions during larger swells. The region offers a remote, undeveloped atmosphere with rich biodiversity and traditional fishing communities.

name: Sao Tome Island
description: Larger main island with occasional surf breaks along its coastline. The capital São Tomé provides the main hub, while the island features volcanic peaks, tropical forests, and beaches. Surf conditions are inconsistent but can produce rideable waves during certain swell windows, particularly on exposed coastlines.

name: Zanzibar North
description: Northern region of Zanzibar island featuring occasional surf breaks along the Indian Ocean coastline. The area includes Nungwi and Kendwa beaches known for their white sand and turquoise waters. Surf conditions are generally inconsistent but can produce rideable waves during monsoon seasons when swells arrive from the Indian Ocean.

name: Manyan
description: Coastal region with limited documented surf breaks. The area features beaches along the Gulf of Guinea, with occasional surfable conditions during larger swells. The region offers a quiet, undeveloped coastline with traditional fishing communities.

name: Bizerte
description: Northern coastal governorate on the Mediterranean with occasional surf breaks influenced by seasonal winds. The region includes historic Bizerte with its old port and fortifications, while the coastline offers beaches that can produce rideable waves during favorable wind conditions.

name: Jendouba
description: Inland governorate in northwestern Tunisia with limited coastal access. The region is primarily known for its mountainous terrain and agricultural areas rather than surf breaks.

name: Nabeul
description: Northeastern coastal governorate featuring Mediterranean beaches with occasional surf conditions. The region includes Hammamet and other resort towns, with surf generally inconsistent but possible during seasonal wind swells.

name: Tunis
description: Capital region and governorate on the Mediterranean coast. The area includes the historic medina and coastal beaches, with occasional surfable conditions during favorable wind patterns. The region serves as the cultural and administrative hub of Tunisia.

name: Bir Ganduz
description: Remote coastal region in northern Western Sahara with sparse population and limited infrastructure. The area features rugged Atlantic coastline with potential for beach and reef breaks, though surf conditions are largely undocumented. The region represents the wild, undeveloped nature of much of Western Sahara's coast.

name: Boujdour
description: Coastal region featuring the Boujdour Lighthouse, a notable landmark on the Atlantic coast. The area includes dramatic cliffs and dunes along the Côte Aftissate-Boujdour, with potential surf breaks though conditions are less documented than southern regions like Dakhla.

name: Dakhla
description: Major surf and kitesurfing destination on the Río de Oro Peninsula, featuring both ocean-side reef and point breaks plus a large lagoon ideal for flat-water sports. The region includes spots like Foum Labouir, Point d'Or, and La Sarga, with consistent winds and swells during winter months. The White Dune is a striking natural landmark, while the lagoon supports oyster farming and water sports tourism.

name: Echdera
description: Remote coastal stretch with limited documentation and sparse population. The region features wild Atlantic coastline with potential surf breaks, though access is difficult and infrastructure minimal.

name: El Aargub
description: Coastal region with rugged Atlantic exposure and potential surf breaks. The area features desert-meets-ocean landscapes with minimal development and challenging access conditions.

name: Foum el Oued
description: Coastal area near Laâyoune featuring La Corniche, an 11-kilometer beach break with mixed rock ledge and sand bottom. The area offers more accessible surf conditions compared to remote southern regions, with beach breaks suitable for intermediate surfers during favorable swell and wind conditions.

name: Itmlily
description: Remote coastal region with limited documentation and sparse infrastructure. The area features wild Atlantic coastline with potential for surf breaks, though conditions and access remain largely unexplored.

name: Laayoune Beach
description: Coastal area near Laâyoune, the largest city in Western Sahara. The region includes beaches along the Atlantic coast with occasional surfable conditions, though surf is less consistent than southern regions like Dakhla. The area serves as an administrative hub with more infrastructure than remote coastal stretches.

name: Uad Guenifa
description: Remote coastal settlement at the extreme southern tip of Western Sahara near the Mauritania border, on the Cabo Blanco peninsula. The region includes surf spots like Cap Blanc and La Gouira, with reef and beach breaks that work during favorable swell conditions. The area is extremely remote with minimal infrastructure, representing one of the most isolated surf frontiers in Western Sahara.

name: Chittagong
description: Coastal division along the Bay of Bengal featuring Cox's Bazar, the world's longest continuous sea beach. Surf conditions are limited and inconsistent, with waves primarily driven by monsoon swells and seasonal storms rather than consistent groundswell. The region offers long sandy beaches suitable for beach walks and fishing, with occasional rideable conditions during favorable wind and swell periods. The area serves as a major tourism hub with vibrant local culture and seafood markets.

name: Brunei-Muara District
description: Coastal district around Bandar Seri Begawan with tropical beaches like Muara and Serasa. Surf potential is minimal, with gentle beach breaks that work sporadically during favorable wind conditions. The region is better known for water sports like kayaking and windsurfing in sheltered bays. Warm waters and calm seas make it suitable for recreational beach activities rather than consistent surf.

name: Kampong Medaras
description: Small coastal village area with limited documented surf breaks. The region features tropical beaches with occasional rideable conditions during larger swells or storm events. Surf infrastructure is minimal, with the area more oriented toward local fishing and beach recreation.

name: Fujian
description: Southeastern coast facing the Taiwan Strait with emerging surf culture. Spots like Liu'ao and Haitan Bay offer clean beach breaks, best from October through May and during typhoon season. The region features both left and right breaks over sand bottoms, suitable for intermediate surfers. Pingtan Island adds to the coastal appeal with kitesurfing opportunities and scenic island landscapes.

name: Guangdong
description: Subtropical south China coast with accessible surf spots near major urban centers. Beaches like Xichong and Dongchong on the Dapeng Peninsula, plus Shuangyue Bay in Huizhou, offer consistent beach breaks suitable for beginners and intermediates. Surf is best during summer months and when typhoon swells arrive. The region combines surf accessibility with proximity to Shenzhen and other Pearl River Delta cities, offering both urban amenities and coastal recreation.

name: Guangxi
description: Coastal region along the Gulf of Tonkin with limited surf development. Beihai's Qiaogang Beach offers occasional surfable conditions during summer months from May to September. The region features warmer waters and quieter beaches compared to more developed surf zones, with surf potential primarily during typhoon remnants or larger storm events. The area maintains a more natural, undeveloped coastal character.

name: Hainan
description: Tropical island province serving as China's premier surf destination. The eastern coast around Wanning features consistent left-hand point breaks like Riyue Bay, with peak surf during the northeast monsoon from November to February. The region hosts international surf events and has developed surf infrastructure, while spots like Houhai and Shimei Bay offer more beginner-friendly beach breaks. Warm waters year-round and a growing surf culture define the island's appeal.

name: Hebei
description: Northern coastal province bordering the Bohai Sea with very limited surf potential. Cold winters and enclosed sea conditions result in weak, inconsistent swell. The region is better known for beach resort tourism, particularly around Beidaihe, rather than surf breaks. Occasional rideable conditions may occur during summer storm events, but surf infrastructure and culture remain minimal.

name: Liaoning
description: Northeastern province along the Yellow Sea and Bohai Bay with marginal surf conditions. Cold waters and limited swell exposure make surfing rare, with occasional beach breaks possible during summer months. The region features coastal resort areas like Dalian, but surf culture and infrastructure are largely undeveloped compared to southern Chinese provinces.

name: Shandong
description: Coastal province on the Yellow Sea with moderate surf potential, particularly around Qingdao. Shilaoren Beach offers beginner-friendly beach breaks that work best during summer and early autumn when wind swells arrive. The region features a mix of sandy beaches and rocky headlands, with surf conditions more consistent than northern provinces but still seasonal. Qingdao serves as the main hub with colonial architecture, beach culture, and growing surf scene.

name: Zhejiang
description: Eastern coast facing the East China Sea with emerging surf spots, especially around Zhoushan Archipelago and Wenzhou. The region features islands, bays, and varied coastline that can produce surf during typhoon season and summer swells. Spots like Zhujiajian offer beach breaks, while the famous Qiantang River tidal bore provides a unique river wave phenomenon. The area combines island scenery, fishing culture, and developing surf infrastructure.

name: Islands District
description: Outlying islands district encompassing Lantau, Lamma, Cheung Chau and numerous smaller islands. Surf potential is limited, with occasional rideable conditions during strong northeast swells or typhoon events. The region offers dramatic coastal scenery, hiking trails, and fishing villages, with beaches better suited for recreational activities than consistent surf. The area provides a rural contrast to Hong Kong's urban core.

name: Sai Kung District
description: Rugged eastern coastline featuring Hong Kong's premier surf destination at Tai Long Wan, a remote bay with four beaches offering left and right beach breaks. The region picks up northeast monsoon and typhoon swells, producing the most consistent surf in Hong Kong. Access requires hiking or boat transport, with unpatrolled beaches and strong rips. The area combines wild coastal scenery with country park trails, making it a destination for both surf and nature enthusiasts.

name: Wan Chai
description: Urban district on Hong Kong Island with very limited surf potential. The coastline is heavily developed with harbour infrastructure, seawalls, and urban beaches. No notable surf breaks exist, with the area better suited for harbour activities and urban recreation rather than wave riding.

name: Andaman and Nicobar
description: Remote archipelago in the Bay of Bengal with tropical beaches and reef breaks. Little Andaman features spots like Butler Bay and Jarawa Point, offering powerful reef breaks best from March to April when winds are lighter. The region requires boat access to many breaks, with warm waters and minimal crowds. Surf conditions suit intermediate to advanced surfers, with reef hazards and strong currents. The islands combine dense jungle, coral reefs, and pristine beaches, creating one of India's most remote surf frontiers.

name: Andhra Pradesh
description: Long eastern coastline along the Bay of Bengal with beaches like Rushikonda and Yarada near Visakhapatnam. Surf conditions are generally gentle and suitable for beginners, with waves primarily during post-monsoon periods. The region features broad sandy beaches, estuaries, and fishing villages, with surf infrastructure less developed compared to Tamil Nadu or Kerala. Swell consistency is moderate, making it better suited for learning and casual surf sessions.

name: Goa
description: Western coast state known for its vibrant surf culture and palm-lined beaches. The dry season from November to April offers consistent beach breaks at spots like Arambol, Palolem, and Agonda, suitable for beginners and intermediates. Monsoon months bring larger but often messy conditions. The region combines relaxed beach vibes, Portuguese colonial architecture, and a well-established surf scene with numerous schools and camps.

name: Karnataka
description: Southwestern coastline with emerging surf spots, particularly around Mulki and the border region with Goa. The area features less crowded beaches and reef breaks compared to Goa, with surf potential during monsoon seasons. The region offers a quieter alternative to Goa's more developed surf scene, with forested coastlines and river mouth breaks that work when Arabian Sea swells arrive.

name: Kerala
description: Tropical southwestern coast featuring one of India's premier surf destinations. Kovalam offers a famous point break at Edakkallu Rock Garden, while Varkala provides consistent beach breaks with cliff-backed scenery. Best surf occurs from October to April when southwest swells are active. The region combines warm waters, reef and beach breaks suitable for all levels, with lush backwaters and coastal temples adding to the area's appeal.

name: Maharashtra
description: Western coast facing the Arabian Sea with limited but emerging surf potential. Spots like Kelwa Beach and Aksa Beach near Mumbai offer weekend surf opportunities, primarily during monsoon seasons. Surf conditions are generally softer beach breaks, less consistent than southern states like Goa or Kerala. The region features rugged Konkan coastline with cliff-backed beaches, though surf infrastructure remains minimal.

name: Odisha
description: Eastern coast along the Bay of Bengal with beaches like Puri and Talasari. Surf conditions are inconsistent, with waves primarily during cyclonic weather and monsoon transitions. The region features wide sandy beaches, river mouths, and strong marine ecosystems, though surf infrastructure is less developed. Surf potential exists but requires favorable storm conditions to produce rideable waves.

name: Tamil Nadu
description: Eastern coast state serving as one of India's premier surf destinations. Covelong near Chennai features a well-established surf village with multiple schools, while Mahabalipuram offers point breaks near ancient temple sites. Best surf occurs during northeast monsoon from September to March. The region combines consistent beach and reef breaks suitable for all levels, with rich cultural heritage and warm waters defining the area's appeal.

name: Union Territory of Puducherry
description: Small coastal union territory surrounded by Tamil Nadu, featuring beaches like Serenity Beach near Auroville. The region offers consistent beach breaks suitable for beginners, with surf schools and a growing surf culture. Conditions are similar to nearby Tamil Nadu, with best surf during northeast monsoon. The area combines mellow surf with the unique cultural atmosphere of Auroville, creating a quieter alternative to Tamil Nadu's more developed spots.

name: West Bengal
description: Northeastern Bay of Bengal coast with very limited surf potential. Beaches like Digha offer gentle waves primarily during cyclonic storms, with surf conditions generally inconsistent. The region features wide tidal ranges, sandy beaches, and the vast Sundarbans delta, though surf culture and infrastructure remain minimal. The area is better suited for beach recreation and water sports rather than consistent wave riding.

name: Aceh
description: Powerful reef and point breaks along the western and northern coasts of Sumatra, featuring spots like Lhoknga and Lampuuk that offer both hollow barrels and more forgiving beach breaks. Offshore islands like the Banyak and Hinako groups deliver world-class reef passes with consistent Indian Ocean swells during dry season from March to October. Waves are best suited for intermediate to advanced surfers, with the area being more remote and less crowded than southern provinces. Rugged coastline and challenging access define the region, creating an adventurous surf experience.

name: Bali
description: Legendary reef breaks like Uluwatu and Padang Padang still deliver world-class barrels, but good luck finding one without sharing it with fifty other surfers and a drone filming someone's morning routine. The Bukit Peninsula remains a mechanical wave factory, while Canggu has transformed into a co-working space with occasional surf breaks, where digital nomads discuss their latest app idea between sets. Traffic jams rival Los Angeles, rental scooters outnumber locals, and the lineups are as crowded as the smoothie bowl cafes. Still, when the swell hits and the wind goes offshore, those perfect lefts remind you why everyone came here in the first place, even if you have to navigate through a sea of GoPros and Instagram stories to get one.

name: Bengkulu
description: Rugged coastline facing the Indian Ocean with limited documented surf breaks. The area features potential for reef and beach breaks during favorable swell conditions, though surf infrastructure is minimal compared to more established regions. The coastline offers exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers seeking uncrowded waves, with breaks that work when swell and wind align.

name: East Nusa Tenggara
description: Island archipelago including Flores, Sumba, and Timor with diverse surf potential across exposed coastlines. Reef breaks and point breaks vary by island and exposure, with Rote Island offering consistent reef breaks while other islands provide more remote and exploratory surf opportunities. Swells arrive from both the Indian Ocean and Pacific, with best conditions during dry season. The region combines world-class surf with dramatic volcanic landscapes, traditional cultures, and pristine marine environments.

name: Central Java
description: Southern coastline with beach breaks and occasional reef points offering surf suitable for intermediate and beginner levels. Spots like Kalipait, Kendalrejo, Sindanglaut, and Tiger Tracks provide varied conditions from mellow beach breaks to more challenging setups. The region receives consistent Indian Ocean swells during dry season, with surf generally less powerful than East Java's heavy reef breaks. The area combines accessible surf with rich Javanese culture, ancient temples, and easier access from major cities.

name: East Java
description: Southern coastline featuring G-Land (Grajagan), one of Indonesia's most famous left-hand reef breaks, alongside spots like Pacitan and Tanjung Kucur. The region receives consistent Indian Ocean swells during dry season, producing powerful waves suitable for intermediate to advanced surfers. Heavy reef barrels dominate the area, with G-Land offering world-class mechanical lefts that break over shallow reef. The region combines world-class surf with volcanic landscapes, traditional Javanese culture, and challenging access to many remote breaks.

name: Maluku
description: Islands in the Banda Sea with limited documented surf breaks. The area includes Ambon and surrounding islands, with potential for reef breaks on exposed coastlines. Surf conditions are less consistent than more established regions, offering exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers. The region combines tropical island scenery with rich spice trade history and diverse marine ecosystems.

name: North Sumatra
description: Legendary surf islands like Nias, Hinako, and Simeulue feature some of Indonesia's most famous breaks. Nias offers Lagundri Bay, one of the country's premier right-hand point breaks, while offshore islands deliver powerful reef passes with long walls and hollow barrels. Consistent Indian Ocean swells arrive during dry season from March to November, with peak conditions in mid-year. The area combines world-class surf with dramatic coastline, traditional cultures, and challenging access to many breaks.

name: South Sumatra
description: Famous Krui region home to legendary left-hand point breaks like Ujung Bocur and Krui Left. The area offers powerful reef breaks and beach breaks, with consistent swells during dry season from April to October when southwest swells dominate. Long left point breaks, hollow barrels, and surf suitable for intermediate to advanced surfers define the region. Krui has developed surf infrastructure with lodges and coastal road access, while maintaining a more laid-back atmosphere than Bali.

name: Ujungdesa
description: Coastal area in East Bali encompassing spots like Pantai Ujung and Jasri Beach in the Karangasem area. Beach breaks and reef points line the eastern coastline, with surf conditions best during dry season when Indian Ocean swells arrive. The area offers a quieter alternative to Bali's more famous southern breaks, with warm waters and scenic coastal landscapes. Distinct from Ujung Genteng in West Java, this represents a localized surf area in East Bali.

name: West Java
description: Extensive southern coastline with world-class surf breaks including legendary spots like Pelabuhan Ratu (Cimaja), Ujung Genteng, Sawarna, and offshore Panaitan Island. Breaks range from mellow beach breaks to hollow reef barrels, with dry season from April to October bringing consistent southwest swells and offshore winds. Diverse breaks suit all levels, with less crowding than Bali and rugged, dramatic coastline. Panaitan Island's One Palm Point ranks among Indonesia's most perfect left-hand reef breaks.

name: West Nusa Tenggara
description: Islands including Lombok and Sumbawa feature world-class reef breaks and consistent surf. Lombok offers powerful reef breaks like Desert Point, one of the world's longest left-handers, while Sumbawa features spots like Lakey Peak and Scar Reef. Consistent Indian Ocean swells arrive with dry season bringing offshore winds on southern coasts. The area combines world-class surf with less crowding than Bali, dramatic volcanic landscapes, and challenging access to many remote breaks.

name: West Sumatra
description: Legendary Mentawai Islands home to some of the world's most consistent and perfect reef breaks. The Mentawais feature world-famous spots like Macaronis, HT's, Lance's Right, and Rifles, offering ultra-consistent, hollow reef waves. The mainland coast also offers beach and reef breaks, though more exposed and less perfect than the islands. Peak season runs from April to October during dry season. The region combines world-class surf with lush tropical backdrop, extensive surf camp infrastructure, and boat-accessible breaks.

name: Yogyakarta
description: Southern coast featuring beach breaks and occasional reef points. Spots like Wediombo, Krakal, and Parangtritis offer surf suitable for intermediate and beginner levels, with less powerful conditions than West Java's reef breaks. Best surf occurs during dry season when Indian Ocean swells arrive. The region combines accessible surf with rich cultural heritage, proximity to ancient temples, and easier travel compared to more remote breaks.

name: Central District
description: Coastal stretch from Netanya to Herzliya featuring consistent beach breaks and jetties that create punchy peaks. The area serves as the primary surf hub with shifting sandbars and stone structures providing reliable waves for local weekend crowds. Surf conditions work best during winter when Mediterranean swells arrive, with spots offering both left and right breaks suitable for intermediate surfers.

name: Haifa
description: Coastal city at the base of Mount Carmel featuring unique reef setups and long jetties that create high-quality rights. The area benefits from a coastline that captures Mediterranean swells differently than southern stretches, with the surf scene centered around iconic breaks. The region offers a more core surf atmosphere compared to the urban centers further south.

name: Northern District
description: Northern coastline stretching toward the Lebanese border with rugged beaches and rocky entries. Breaks near Nahariya and Akko offer a mix of rocky points and beach peaks that often hold more size when winter west swells hit the coast. The area is less crowded than Tel Aviv, providing more space and consistent conditions during favorable swell windows.

name: Southern District
description: Vast coastline from Ashdod down to the Gaza border featuring wide-open beaches with significant fetch. The area produces some of the most powerful, hollow beach breaks in the country when wind stays offshore, though conditions can be fickle. Sandbars create quality peaks with plenty of room to spread out, making it ideal for surfers seeking less crowded lineups.

name: Tel Aviv
description: Mediterranean surf city where surfing is woven into the urban fabric. From fashionable crowds at Hilton Beach to more democratic peaks, the area is crowded, chaotic, and fiercely local. The city offers consistent beach breaks that work year-round, with best conditions during winter swells. No better place to experience Israeli surf culture and post-session street food.

name: Tel Baruch
description: Northern Tel Aviv beach break known for being more sheltered and mellow than the city center. The spot serves as a go-to for longboarders and beginners, providing consistent, softer waves that work well on mid-sized swells that might be closing out elsewhere. The area offers a more relaxed alternative to the intense lineups of central Tel Aviv.

name: Aichi
description: Irago Peninsula catches plenty of Pacific swell with Long Beach serving as the go-to spot. Consistent peaks attract Nagoya crowds on weekends, offering reliable beach breaks that work during favorable swell conditions.

name: Akita
description: Sea of Japan coastline where winter is the prime season. Expect freezing winds and chunky, short-period swells that require thick wetsuits and determination. The area offers raw, uncrowded setups for those willing to brave the cold.

name: Aomori
description: Northern tip of Honshu offering raw, uncrowded setups on both Pacific and Sea of Japan coasts. The area provides solitude over convenience, with breaks that work during favorable swell windows when conditions align.

name: Chiba
description: Heavyweight champion of Japanese surfing featuring high-performance peaks at Shidashita and heavy beach breaks at Wada. The most consistent and crowded zone in the country, with breaks that work year-round when swells arrive.

name: Fukui
description: Rugged stretch of Sea of Japan coast serving as a winter playground for those escaping flat spells on the Pacific side. Rocky points and reefs wake up during northwest storms, offering quality setups when conditions align.

name: Fukuoka
description: Genkai Sea provides the goods with breaks around Itoshima offering surprisingly fun beach breaks when cold fronts blow through. Largely a winter destination with conditions that work during favorable wind patterns.

name: Fukushima
description: World-class Kitaizumi beach break known for punchy, powerful waves. The surf community here is incredibly resilient, with waves remaining some of the best and punchiest on the east coast.

name: Hokkaido
description: Northern island with raw, cold-water surf potential. The area features exposed coastlines that can handle massive swells, though conditions are challenging and require serious cold-water gear. Best during winter months when Pacific storms generate powerful waves.

name: Ibaraki
description: Chiba colder, grittier northern neighbor that handles large swells better than most beach breaks. Very consistent with powerful waves, though water temperature keeps sessions shorter during winter months.

name: Ishikawa
description: Noto Peninsula acts as a giant swell magnet in the Sea of Japan. Chirihama is famous for its drivable beach, but hidden rocky nooks offer the real quality when winter swells arrive.

name: Iwate
description: Rugged Sanriku coast is deep-water territory that can handle massive Pacific swells that would wash out other regions. Beautiful and raw, not for the faint of heart, with powerful waves during storm events.

name: Kagoshima
description: Southern Kyushu coastline with potential for reef and beach breaks. The area receives swells from the Pacific, though surf conditions are less consistent than northern prefectures. Best during typhoon season when larger swells arrive.

name: Kanagawa
description: Welcome to Shonan, the spiritual heart of Japanese surf culture. Iconic and stylish, though flat more often than not. When typhoon swells actually hit, expect massive crowds at every peak.

name: Kochi
description: Jewel of Shikoku featuring pristine river mouths and heavy reef breaks. The locals are protective for good reason, so bring respect and solid rail-game. Famous spots work during typhoon swells and winter storms.

name: Kumamoto
description: Mostly shielded by other islands, though the Amakusa area can produce fun waves during typhoon season or strong western swells. A quiet alternative to busier Kyushu spots, with breaks that work when conditions align.

name: Kyoto
description: Northern coast has legit Sea of Japan quality with winter beach breaks offering clean, hollow conditions when wind lightens up. Forget the temples, the surf here is the real draw for those in the know.

name: Mie
description: Ise-Shima region home to Ichigo-hama (Strawberry Beach), a popular, fun beach break that works well on small to mid-sized southeast swells. Very scenic and relatively mellow compared to more powerful regions.

name: Miyagi
description: Sendai serves as the hub with wide, sandy beaches that pick up plenty of east swell. Dedicated surf community with plenty of power in the waves, offering consistent conditions during favorable swell windows.

name: Miyazaki
description: Tropical vibes, warm water, and the most consistent surf in Japan. Whether long logs at Kizaki-hama or heavy tubes at Uchiumi, this is a year-round paradise with breaks that work across all seasons.

name: Nagasaki
description: Complex coastline with many islands requiring specific swell directions to wrap in. During typhoons, it offers some of the most unique and uncrowded setups in Kyushu, though conditions are fickle.

name: Niigata
description: Long stretch of Sea of Japan coast where winter is peak season. Beach breaks and river mouths can get surprisingly good during frequent cold fronts, offering variety when conditions align.

name: Okinawa
description: True tropical reef surfing with powerful, shallow, tide-dependent waves. Bring booties and big-wave guns for typhoon season, when the area produces some of Japan most challenging and rewarding surf.

name: Saga
description: Small but mighty Sea of Japan access with Karatsu serving as the main zone. Fun waves during winter months for the local Kyushu crew, offering consistent beach breaks when swells arrive.

name: Shimane
description: Raw Sea of Japan energy that is often windy and wild. When conditions clean up, the points and beaches here are among the best in western Japan, offering quality setups during winter swells.

name: Shizuoka
description: From massive Shizunami beach to windy points of Omaezaki, this is a major surf hub. Gets very crowded, but sheer variety of spots means there is usually something breaking when swells arrive.

name: Tokushima
description: Legendary Kaifu river mouth that when firing is arguably the best wave in the country. A perfect, heavy, spinning right tube that demands respect. Watch from the bridge if you are not an expert.

name: Tokyo
description: Surfing here means jumping on a ferry to the Izu Islands. Niijima is the standout featuring Habushiura, a world-class beach break with heavy, turquoise barrels and white sand that works during typhoon swells.

name: Tottori
description: Heart of the San in coast offering high-quality beach breaks that are a favorite for Kansai surfers during winter. Fast, hollow, and cold, with breaks that work when northwest swells arrive.

name: Toyama
description: Located deep in Toyama Bay, it needs massive swell to get past the Noto Peninsula. When it does, local river mouths offer short but intense sessions with powerful waves during favorable conditions.

name: Wakayama
description: Isoko is the main draw, a massive, mellow beach break that is a longboarder dream. Primary escape for Osaka crowds, offering consistent waves when swells arrive from the Pacific.

name: Yamagata
description: Another winter gem on the Sea of Japan featuring quality reefs and river mouths that remain relatively uncrowded compared to spots further south. Breaks work during northwest swells when conditions align.

name: Yamaguchi
description: With access to both Sea of Japan and Seto Inland Sea, the northern coast is the winner. Fun, playful beach breaks during winter northwest swell cycle, offering consistent conditions when winds are favorable.

name: El Madfoûn
description: Coastal area with limited documented surf breaks along the Mediterranean. The region features potential for beach breaks during favorable wind and swell conditions, though surf infrastructure remains minimal. The area offers exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers seeking uncrowded waves when Mediterranean swells align.

name: Mohafazat Liban-Nord
description: Northern governorate featuring Mediterranean coastline with occasional surf breaks. The area receives swells primarily during winter months when storms generate waves along the coast. Surf conditions are inconsistent, with breaks working when wind and swell patterns align favorably.

name: Mohafazat Mont-Liban
description: Central governorate encompassing Beirut and surrounding coastal areas. The region features the most developed surf scene in Lebanon, with beach breaks that work during winter swells. The area combines urban access with occasional rideable conditions when Mediterranean storms generate waves.

name: South Governorate
description: Southern governorate including Tyre and coastal stretches along the Mediterranean. The region offers beach breaks that work during winter swells, with conditions best when wind patterns are favorable. The area features a growing surf community with breaks suitable for intermediate surfers.

name: Johor
description: Southern state on the Malay Peninsula with limited surf potential due to sheltered coastline. The region faces the Strait of Malacca and South China Sea, with surf conditions primarily during monsoon seasons. Breaks are inconsistent, offering occasional rideable waves when swells arrive.

name: Kelantan
description: Northeastern state on the Malay Peninsula with coastline facing the South China Sea. The region receives swells during northeast monsoon from November to March, with beach breaks working when conditions align. Surf infrastructure is minimal, offering exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers.

name: Pahang
description: Eastern state on the Malay Peninsula with extensive coastline along the South China Sea. The region features beach breaks that work during northeast monsoon season, with spots like Cherating offering consistent waves. The area combines surf opportunities with tropical rainforest and laid-back coastal towns.

name: Sabah
description: Eastern Malaysian state on Borneo featuring coastline along the Sulu Sea and Celebes Sea. The region offers reef breaks and beach breaks, with spots working during monsoon seasons. The area combines world-class surf potential with pristine tropical islands, though access to many breaks requires boat transport.

name: Sarawak
description: Western Malaysian state on Borneo with limited surf potential due to sheltered coastline. The region faces the South China Sea, with surf conditions primarily during monsoon seasons. Breaks are inconsistent, offering occasional rideable waves when swells wrap around the coastline.

name: Terengganu
description: Northeastern state on the Malay Peninsula featuring the most consistent surf in mainland Malaysia. The region receives reliable swells during northeast monsoon from November to March, with spots like Rantau Abang offering quality beach breaks. The area combines consistent surf with traditional fishing villages and tropical coastline.

name: Addu Atoll
description: Located at the southernmost edge of the archipelago, this atoll catches swells that other regions miss, producing powerful reef breaks with less competition for waves. The area requires longer boat journeys from Male, keeping crowds minimal while delivering consistent Indian Ocean energy during southwest monsoon season. Breaks work best from May through October, with warm waters and pristine tropical setting defining the remote surf experience.

name: Dhaalu Atholhu
description: Known for its fast, hollow right-hand reef breaks that barrel over shallow coral formations. The atoll features multiple quality passes that handle larger swells, with waves breaking close to the reef requiring precise positioning. Accessible by boat from nearby atolls, the area offers less crowded lineups than North Male while maintaining consistent surf during peak season.

name: Furanafushi
description: A small island break within North Male Atoll that produces quality left-handers when swell direction aligns. The spot works during southwest monsoon, offering a more relaxed alternative to the atoll's famous breaks. Warm waters and easy access from Male make this a popular choice for intermediate surfers seeking less intense waves.

name: Gaafu Alif Atoll
description: One of the southernmost atolls featuring long, mechanical left-hand reef breaks that peel for extended distances. The region catches consistent swells with multiple breaks working simultaneously, though access requires longer boat trips. Less developed than northern atolls, the area provides uncrowded perfection for experienced surfers willing to travel further.

name: Gaafu Dhaalu Atoll
description: Famous for producing some of the Maldives' longest and most perfect left-hand waves, with breaks that can run for hundreds of meters. The atoll's reef passes create mechanical, barreling waves that work consistently during southwest monsoon season. Remote location means fewer crowds, though accessing the breaks requires dedicated boat charters from Male.

name: Isdhoo
description: A local island break in Laamu Atoll that works when swell wraps around the atoll's geography. The spot offers both left and right-hand waves depending on conditions, with a more relaxed atmosphere than resort-accessible breaks. The area combines quality surf with authentic Maldivian island culture, providing a different experience from the typical surf resort scene.

name: Kaafu Atoll
description: Home to legendary breaks like Sultans, Pasta Point, Cokes, Chickens, and Jailbreaks that have defined Maldives surfing for decades. These world-class reef passes produce ultra-consistent, hollow waves during southwest monsoon from May to October, with mechanical lefts and rights breaking over shallow coral. Proximity to Male makes this the most accessible surf region, though popularity means crowded lineups at famous breaks. Extensive surf infrastructure includes live-aboard boats and resort access, creating the country's most developed surf scene.

name: Kudarikilu
description: An outer atoll break that catches swells before they reach more protected areas, producing powerful waves when conditions align. The spot works best during larger swells when other breaks become too crowded, offering experienced surfers challenging conditions over reef. Access requires boat transport, with the break offering a more adventurous alternative to well-documented spots.

name: Laamu Atholhu
description: Positioned in the central archipelago, this atoll features a mix of left and right-hand reef breaks that work during different swell directions. The area offers less documented breaks that reward exploration, with quality waves breaking over coral formations. Boat access from nearby atolls makes this a good option for surfers seeking uncrowded perfection away from the main surf routes.

name: Maabaidhoo
description: A specific island break known for producing quality waves when swell and wind conditions combine favorably. The spot works during southwest monsoon season, offering both left and right-hand options depending on the break's orientation. Less crowded than famous breaks, the area provides a more intimate surf experience with warm waters and tropical backdrop.

name: Maamendhoo
description: An island break that produces consistent waves during peak swell season, with both left and right-hand options available. The spot works best when southwest swells arrive, creating quality surf over reef formations. Accessible by boat, the break offers less competition than more famous spots while maintaining reliable conditions.

name: World
description: One of the Maldives' most famous and consistent breaks, producing long, perfect left-hand waves that barrel over shallow reef. The break works virtually every day during southwest monsoon season, creating mechanical waves that peel for extended distances. Located in North Male Atoll, the spot is easily accessible by boat from Male, though its reputation for perfection means crowded lineups are common. The wave breaks close to the reef, requiring precise positioning and experience with shallow coral breaks.

name: Noonu Atoll
description: Positioned in the northern reaches of the archipelago, this atoll catches early swells and produces quality reef breaks with less documented spots. The region offers exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers, with breaks that work when swell direction and wind align. Less developed surf infrastructure means more adventure and fewer crowds, though accessing breaks requires boat charters.

name: Scuba Dive Centre, Guraidhoo Male Atoll
description: Located on Guraidhoo Island in North Male Atoll, this break is accessible from the island's dive center location. The spot produces quality waves during southwest monsoon season, with both left and right-hand options depending on conditions. Easy access from Male and the island's infrastructure make this a convenient option, though the break can get crowded during peak season.

name: Thaa Atholhu
description: Featuring multiple reef passes that produce both left and right-hand waves, this central atoll offers variety for surfers seeking different wave types. The region catches consistent swells during southwest monsoon, with breaks working at different stages of the tide. Less crowded than North Male Atoll, the area provides quality surf with more space in the lineup and authentic Maldivian island atmosphere.

name: Rakhine State
description: Bay of Bengal coastline featuring Ngapali Beach, one of Myanmar's most well-known surf destinations. The region receives swells during monsoon season from May to September, with beach breaks that work when conditions align. Surf infrastructure remains minimal, though the area offers exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers seeking uncrowded waves along the country's western coast.

name: Tanintharyi Region
description: Southern region bordering Thailand with Andaman Sea exposure offering potential for reef and beach breaks. The area receives swells during monsoon season, though surf documentation is limited and access can be challenging. The region combines potential surf opportunities with pristine tropical coastline and less developed tourism infrastructure compared to neighboring countries.

name: Hamnam
description: Coastal area with extremely limited documented surf activity due to government restrictions and access limitations. The region faces the Sea of Japan, with potential for waves when swells arrive, though independent travel and surf exploration are not permitted. The area remains one of the world's most inaccessible surf destinations.

name: Sokto-ri
description: Eastern coastal location with virtually no public surf documentation or access. The area faces the Sea of Japan and likely receives swells, though government restrictions prevent independent surf travel. The region represents one of surfing's final frontiers, with conditions that remain largely unknown to the international surf community.

name: Southeastern Governorate
description: Arabian Sea coastline with limited documented surf breaks, though the region receives swells during monsoon seasons. The area offers potential for beach and reef breaks when conditions align, with warm waters and tropical setting. Surf infrastructure is minimal, providing exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers willing to navigate the region's less developed surf scene.

name: Bicol
description: Home to Catanduanes, one of the Philippines' most consistent surf destinations with powerful reef breaks that work year-round. The region faces the Pacific Ocean, receiving swells from multiple directions that create quality waves at spots like Majestics and Puraran. The area combines world-class surf with dramatic coastline, though access requires boat transport to many breaks. Peak season runs during typhoon season from July to October when larger swells arrive.

name: Cagayan Valley
description: Northern Luzon region with Pacific Ocean exposure producing quality surf during typhoon season. The area receives powerful swells from July to October, with beach breaks and reef points working when conditions align. Less documented than southern regions, the area offers exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers seeking uncrowded waves along the northern coastline.

name: Calabarzon
description: Southern Luzon region encompassing areas like Quezon with Pacific-facing coastline. The region receives swells during typhoon season, producing quality waves at various breaks along the coast. Less crowded than more famous destinations, the area combines accessible surf with proximity to Manila, making it a convenient option for weekend surf trips.

name: Damn
description: Coastal area with limited documented surf breaks along the Philippine coastline. The region offers potential for waves during typhoon season when swells arrive, though surf infrastructure remains minimal. The area provides exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers seeking uncrowded conditions.

name: Central Luzon
description: Central region of Luzon island with Pacific Ocean exposure on eastern coasts. The area receives swells during typhoon season from July to October, producing quality waves at various breaks. The region combines surf opportunities with easier access from Manila compared to more remote destinations, though surf infrastructure varies by location.

name: Davao
description: Mindanao region facing the Pacific Ocean with powerful reef breaks that work during typhoon season. The area receives consistent swells from July to October, producing quality waves at various breaks along the coast. Less developed than northern surf destinations, the region offers uncrowded perfection for experienced surfers willing to explore Mindanao's surf potential.

name: Eastern Visayas
description: Island group including Samar and Leyte with extensive Pacific Ocean exposure. The region receives powerful swells during typhoon season, producing quality reef and beach breaks. Spots like Calicoan Island offer consistent waves, with the area combining world-class surf potential with less crowding than more famous destinations. Access to many breaks requires boat transport, creating an adventurous surf experience.

name: Ilocos
description: Northern Luzon coastline facing the Pacific Ocean with quality surf during typhoon season. The region receives powerful swells from July to October, producing consistent waves at various breaks along the coast. The area combines accessible surf with rich cultural heritage, though surf infrastructure remains less developed than southern destinations.

name: Mimaropa
description: Island region including Mindoro, Marinduque, Romblon, and Palawan with varied ocean exposures. The region features both Pacific-facing breaks that work during typhoon season and South China Sea exposure on western coasts. The area offers diverse surf opportunities, with Palawan providing more sheltered conditions while Mindoro receives Pacific swells. Access varies by island, creating an exploratory surf experience across the archipelago.

name: Kaliningrad
description: Baltic Sea exposure producing cold-water surf with beach breaks that work during autumn and winter storms. The region receives swells from the North Sea and Atlantic, creating quality waves when wind and swell patterns align. Cold water requires wetsuits year-round, with surf conditions best from September through March. The area combines surf opportunities with European accessibility, though surf infrastructure remains less developed than warmer destinations.

name: Kamchatka
description: One of the world's most remote surf destinations, this Pacific peninsula features powerful and consistent breaks that work year-round in frigid conditions. The region receives swells from the North Pacific, producing quality waves at various reef and beach breaks. Access requires expensive flights from Moscow, though the area offers uncrowded perfection for experienced cold-water surfers. Wetsuits and booties are essential year-round in these extreme conditions.

name: Krasnodar
description: Warmest surf waters in Russia along the Black Sea, with beach breaks that work during autumn and winter when storms generate waves. The region receives swells from the Black Sea, producing quality waves from September through April. The area combines surf opportunities with resort infrastructure and warmer water temperatures compared to other Russian surf destinations, making it the country's most developed surf region.

name: Krasnodar Krai
description: Administrative region encompassing Russia's primary surf destination along the Black Sea coast. The area receives swells during autumn and winter months, producing quality waves when wind and swell patterns align. The region combines surf opportunities with resort infrastructure, warmer water temperatures, and easier access than remote Pacific destinations, creating Russia's most accessible surf experience.

name: Primorye
description: Vladivostok area featuring cold-water surf with breaks that work during autumn and winter when storms arrive. The region receives swells from the Sea of Japan and Pacific, producing quality waves at various beach and reef breaks. Cold water requires wetsuits year-round, with surf conditions best from September through April. The area offers exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers willing to brave the cold conditions.

name: Sakhalin Oblast
description: Massive island in the Sea of Okhotsk with cold-water surf potential along Pacific-facing coasts. The region receives swells from the North Pacific, producing quality waves when conditions align, though access is challenging and surf infrastructure is minimal. Cold water and remote location make this an adventurous destination for experienced cold-water surfers seeking uncrowded waves in extreme conditions.

name: Busan
description: Serving as South Korea's primary surf hub, this major port city offers consistent beach breaks and jetties along the southeast coast. The area receives swells from the Sea of Japan, producing quality waves year-round with peak conditions during typhoon season from July to October. The city combines surf opportunities with urban infrastructure, making it the country's most accessible and developed surf destination with multiple breaks working in different conditions.

name: Chungcheongnam-do
description: Yellow Sea exposure creating limited surf potential due to sheltered coastline and minimal swell activity. The region receives occasional swells during typhoon season, though breaks are inconsistent and surf infrastructure is minimal. The area offers exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers when conditions align, though it remains less developed than east coast destinations.

name: Gangwon-do
description: Home to South Korea's most famous surf destination with quality beach breaks that work year-round along the east coast. The region receives swells from the Sea of Japan, producing consistent waves at spots like Yangyang and Jukdo Beach. Peak season runs during typhoon season from July to October when larger swells arrive, though the area offers surfable conditions throughout the year. The province combines world-class surf with mountain backdrop and developing surf infrastructure.

name: Gyeongsangbuk-do
description: Northeastern positioning along the Sea of Japan offering quality surf during typhoon season. The region receives swells from July to October, producing consistent waves at various beach breaks along the coast. Less crowded than Gangwon-do, the area offers quality surf with less competition in the lineup, though surf infrastructure remains less developed than more famous destinations.

name: Jeju-do
description: Volcanic island producing South Korea's most consistent and powerful surf, with breaks that work year-round off the southern coast. The region receives swells from multiple directions, creating quality waves at spots like Jungmun Beach and various reef breaks. Typhoon season from July to October brings the largest swells, though the island offers consistent surf throughout the year. The area combines world-class surf with tropical island setting and well-developed surf infrastructure.

name: Jeollanam-do
description: Sheltered Yellow Sea coastline creating limited surf potential with minimal swell exposure. The region receives occasional swells during typhoon season, though breaks are inconsistent and surf infrastructure is minimal. The area offers exploratory opportunities for experienced surfers when conditions align, though it remains less developed than east coast and island destinations.

name: Eastern Province
description: Home to Arugam Bay, Sri Lanka's premier east coast surf destination and one of the country's most iconic surf towns. The region features world-class right-hand point breaks including Main Point with its fast barrels and beginner-friendly inside section, Whiskey Point to the north, Peanut Farm, and the remote Okanda break with punchy bowly rights. Sandy bottom breaks offer forgiving conditions for all skill levels, while the area maintains a laid-back atmosphere reminiscent of 1970s Bali. Best conditions occur from May to September when trade winds turn offshore and Indian Ocean swells push in, with peak consistency in July and August.

name: Province of Uva
description: Inland province in Sri Lanka's central highlands with no coastal access. The region encompasses mountainous terrain including the Haputale Mountains and Ella Gap, featuring tea plantations and elevations ranging from 348 to 1,443 meters above sea level. As a landlocked province, Uva has no surf breaks or ocean access.

name: Southern Province
description: Sri Lanka's primary south coast surf region featuring the country's best beginner-friendly breaks and established surf infrastructure. Weligama offers a massive 3.2-kilometer bay with sand-bottomed beach breaks providing long, protected waves ideal for learning, while Mirissa delivers reef break action for more advanced surfers. The coastline includes Hikkaduwa with consistent 2 to 5-foot surf, Midigama with reef breaks like Ram's and Coconuts, and The Rock at Kabalana. The region receives consistent southwest swells from October to March, with water temperatures staying around 28 degrees Celsius year-round. Numerous surf schools, board rentals, and accessible breaks make this the most developed surf zone in Sri Lanka.

name: Fukien
description: Western coastal region of Taiwan with limited documented surf breaks. The area receives primarily windswell conditions rather than consistent groundswells, with waves typically generated during typhoon events when larger swells arrive from the Taiwan Strait. Surf conditions are generally inconsistent compared to Taiwan's more established east coast breaks.

name: Kaohsiung
description: Major port city in southern Taiwan featuring several accessible surf breaks. Cijin Island offers waves reaching 3 to 4 feet, while West Bay produces smaller 2 to 3 foot conditions rated fair to good. The region serves as a gateway to more established breaks including Tawu, Kenting, and Nanwan Beach, all within 55 miles. Water temperatures remain warm year-round, making it suitable for surfing without wetsuits.

name: Taiwan
description: Island nation featuring Taiwan's most consistent surf region on the east coast around Taitung. The area includes premier breaks like Jinzun Harbor, Donghe, Jihui, Changbin, and Jiqi, offering diverse conditions from beginner-friendly sandy beach breaks at Wushibi to challenging reef setups at Eight Immortals Cave and Chenggong. Best conditions occur during the northeast monsoon from October to April, while typhoon season from June to September produces head-high to double overhead waves with barreling sections. The continental shelf extends 50 to 60 meters with flat seafloor terrain creating smooth, rideable waves. Water temperatures stay between 25 and 30 degrees Celsius year-round, eliminating the need for wetsuits.

name: Changwat Krabi
description: Andaman Sea province with limited direct surf breaks, though nearby options exist within 30 to 44 miles including Koh Lanta's Khlong Dao, Bang Tao, and Nai Yang Reef. The region's coastline features dramatic limestone karsts and pristine beaches, but surf infrastructure is less developed compared to Phuket and Phang Nga provinces to the north. Best conditions align with Thailand's primary surf season from May to October when southwest monsoons bring consistent swells.

name: Changwat Phangnga
description: Home to Khao Lak Beach, one of Thailand's premier surfing destinations located within Khao Lak-Lam Ru National Park. The 9-kilometer beach features waves at ideal heights with a surf school available for beginners. The area experiences two distinct seasons: a low season from May to September with strong waves and monsoon rains, and a high season from October to April with calmer conditions and clear water, though occasional large waves still occur. Additional breaks include Bang Niang Rivermouth and Cape Pakarang, with the region serving as the birthplace of Thai surfing.

name: Changwat Ranong
description: Northern Andaman Sea province featuring Koh Phayam, one of Thailand's most popular surfing destinations.
