The island offers a sandy beach break with stable waves suitable for both beginners and advanced surfers, maintaining beautiful, undisturbed natural surroundings. Unique among Thai surf spots, Koh Phayam has two distinct surfing seasons: October to December and April to May, with the October to December period offering waves when nearby Phuket and Phang Nga spots are typically flat. Access requires travel via Ranong Airport followed by car and boat transfer to the island.

name: Changwat Rayong
description: Features Mae Ramphung Beach, a reef break suitable for beginners with both right and left-hand waves over a sand bottom. The area receives surf approximately 10 months of the year with moderate wave quality, while Mae Phim Beach offers inconsistent beach break conditions that occasionally produce rideable waves during winter months with south swell and northeast offshore winds. The region includes surf infrastructure with Thai Surf School and Rayong Surf Shop providing equipment hire, though conditions are generally less consistent than Andaman Sea breaks to the west.

name: Changwat Surat Thani
description: Encompasses Koh Samui, where an exposed beach break works inconsistently with low reliability. The best conditions occur during the November to April wet season, with February offering the most consistent waves though clean, rideable conditions only occur about 5 percent of the time. Ideal conditions require east-southeast swell with west-northwest offshore winds, producing both left and right-handers when waves do arrive. Nearby alternatives include Choeng Mon Beach on Koh Samui and Chaloklum Beach on Koh Pha Ngan, which may offer better wave potential during poor conditions at the main break.

name: Chanthaburi
description: Eastern coastal region with limited documented surf infrastructure along the Gulf. The area features coastal access, but surf conditions are generally inconsistent and less developed compared to more established Thai surf destinations. Current sea conditions typically show small surf in the 0.1 to 0.2 meter range with calm waters, making it more suitable for other water activities than dedicated surfing.

name: Chon Buri
description: Home to Pattaya and Jomtien Beach, popular tourist destinations that occasionally produce surfable conditions. The area currently experiences waves in the 0.5 to 0.7 meter range with warm water temperatures around 29 degrees Celsius year-round. While the region is better known for windsurfing, jet skiing, and other water sports, occasional surf conditions occur, particularly during favorable wind and swell patterns. The area offers extensive amenities and easy access from Bangkok via major highways.

name: Nakhon Si Thammarat
description: Southern coastal region with limited established surf breaks along the Gulf. The area features coastal access, but surf conditions are generally inconsistent with current forecasts showing small surf in the 0.1 to 0.2 meter range and calm waters. The region is less developed for surfing compared to Thailand's more established Andaman Sea destinations, making it more suitable for other coastal activities than dedicated surf trips.

name: Phetchaburi
description: Coastal region with access to the Gulf but limited documented surf infrastructure. Current sea conditions typically show small surf in the 0.2 to 0.3 meter range with calm waters, indicating inconsistent wave conditions. The area is less developed for surfing compared to Thailand's primary surf destinations on the Andaman Sea coast, making it more suitable for general beach activities than dedicated surfing.

name: Phuket
description: Thailand's most developed surf destination on the Andaman Sea, featuring multiple breaks including Kata Beach, Surin Beach, Patong Beach, Kamala Beach, and Kalim. Kata Beach offers an exposed beach break that works best from May to October with southwest swell and northeast offshore winds, while Surin Beach provides similar conditions with November offering the most consistency at 38 percent clean waves. Additional spots include Karon Beach, Kata Noi Beach, Relax Beach, and Pansea Beach. The region receives consistent swells during the southwest monsoon from May to October, with waves averaging 2 to 4 feet and occasional swells reaching 6 to 9 feet, ideal for beginner and intermediate surfers. Phuket is considered the only location in Thailand suitable for a proper surf trip with nascent surf infrastructure.

name: Prachuap Khiri Khan
description: Gulf of Thailand province featuring Khao Takiab near Hua Hin, a popular surf spot offering conditions suitable for both beginner and experienced surfers. The break features pristine beaches with rolling waves and panoramic views of the Gulf, with local surf instructors available for lessons. The best surfing season runs from November to December when conditions are most favorable, though the area offers beachside dining and various water activities beyond surfing. The region includes several other nearby breaks in the Gulf of Thailand area.

name: Satan
description: Southern Andaman Sea province bordering Malaysia, featuring nearly 100 islands including Koh Tarutao, Koh Lipe, and Koh Bulon Lae. The region has a primarily Muslim population and maintains pristine natural surroundings, but surf infrastructure is less developed compared to more established Thai surf destinations like Phuket and Koh Phayam. While the province offers beautiful coastal scenery and island access, it is not highlighted as a major surfing destination, with better surf conditions typically found further north along the Andaman coast.

name: Antalya
description: Mediterranean coastal region featuring Lara Beach, an exposed sandbar break offering both left and right-hand waves. The area experiences inconsistent, unreliable conditions with winter being the best season when south-southwest swells combine with north offshore winds. Additional breaks in the region include Side West Beach, Patara, Beymelek, Karaoz, and Damlatas Beach, with Alanya particularly popular during winter and spring months when waves can reach up to 20 feet. The Mediterranean coast offers warmer water temperatures around 29.5 degrees Celsius, though windswells are more common than groundswells in this area.

name: Bartın
description: Black Sea province with active surfing conditions for approximately 280 days per year along the scenic coastal road. The region features lush landscapes with sandy beaches and dense forests, maintaining minimal tourism infrastructure compared to Mediterranean alternatives. The Black Sea receives swells from two different directions with no tidal influence, though the relatively fresh water mixing with Mediterranean saltwater reduces buoyancy compared to typical ocean surfing. The area remains largely undeveloped for surfing with numerous unexplored breaks.

name: Hatay
description: Southern Mediterranean region home to the Hatay Surf Center, a non-profit organization providing free surf lessons and mentorship to youth. The area hosts surf festivals across Samandağ, Defne, and Antakya districts, bringing together participants for training combined with art workshops and sports competitions. The Mediterranean coast offers warm water temperatures averaging 65 to 70 degrees Fahrenheit in winter, with wave heights typically ranging from 1 to 12 feet. The region experiences consistent waves particularly suitable for winter surfing, with average swell periods of 6 to 7 seconds.

name: Istanbul
description: Major city spanning the Bosphorus with access to both Black Sea and Marmara Sea coasts. The Black Sea side features Rumeli Feneri, Turkey's biggest surfing hub though heavily localized, along with Merkez Sile offering the longest waves in Turkey extending up to 300 meters on good days. Additional breaks include Aglayankaya, a beachbreak with reliable waves and minimal crowds, and Kumcagiz, a sandbar suitable for all skill levels. The Black Sea coast provides the most consistent and accessible waves in Turkey, with water temperatures getting cold in winter.

name: Kastamonu
description: Northern Black Sea province with coastal access to Turkey's less developed surf region. The area features scenic coastal landscapes with sandy beaches and forested terrain, maintaining minimal surf infrastructure compared to more established Mediterranean destinations. The Black Sea coast receives wind-generated swells with heights ranging from 1 to 12 feet, though surfing remains less developed than the Mediterranean side. The region maintains a small local surfer community with numerous unexplored breaks along the coastal road.

name: Kocaeli
description: Province near Istanbul with access to both Marmara Sea and Black Sea coasts. The region benefits from proximity to Turkey's primary surf hub at Rumeli Feneri on the Black Sea side, though the area itself has limited documented surf breaks. The Marmara Sea side offers calmer conditions more suitable for other water activities, while the Black Sea access provides potential for wind-generated swells. The region serves as a gateway between Istanbul's established surf scene and the less explored northern Black Sea coast.

name: Kirklareli
description: Northeastern region bordering Bulgaria with access to the Black Sea coast. The area features Igne Ada, a sandbar near the Bulgarian border with waves up to 5 feet, easily accessible from the town of Igneada. The region benefits from Black Sea swells with active surfing conditions for approximately 280 days per year, though surf infrastructure remains minimal. The area maintains scenic coastal landscapes with sandy beaches and forested terrain, offering uncrowded conditions compared to more developed Mediterranean destinations.

name: Mersin
description: Mediterranean coastal province with access to Turkey's southern surf region. The area experiences warm water temperatures and consistent waves particularly suitable for winter surfing, with wave heights typically ranging from 1 to 12 feet. The Mediterranean coast offers average swell periods of 6 to 7 seconds, though swells can reach 11 to 12 seconds in some areas. The region maintains less developed surf infrastructure compared to more established destinations like Alanya, though the area offers potential for exploration along the scenic Mediterranean coastline.

name: Ordu
description: Black Sea province featuring river mouth setups including Kepa's River, a perfect wave that works in northerly swells. The region experiences active surfing conditions for approximately 280 days per year along the scenic coastal road, with the Black Sea receiving swells from two different directions and no tidal influence. The area features lush landscapes with sandy beaches and dense forests, maintaining minimal tourism infrastructure. The relatively fresh water mixing with Mediterranean saltwater reduces buoyancy compared to typical ocean surfing, though the region offers numerous unexplored breaks.

name: Synopsis
description: Region with limited documented surf information. The area may have coastal access, but specific surf breaks and conditions are not well established in available sources. Further research would be needed to determine surf potential and infrastructure in this region.

name: Zonguldak
description: Western Black Sea province with coastal access to Turkey's less developed surf region. The area features scenic coastal landscapes along the Black Sea, with active surfing conditions possible for approximately 280 days per year. The Black Sea receives swells from two different directions with no tidal influence, though the relatively fresh water reduces buoyancy compared to typical ocean surfing. The region maintains minimal surf infrastructure with numerous unexplored breaks along the coastal road, offering uncrowded conditions for those willing to explore.

name: Izmir
description: Aegean coastal region featuring Alaçatı in Çeşme, Turkey's windsurfing capital and premier destination for wind-powered water sports. The protected bay features a sandy bottom with shallow waters 200 to 300 meters deep, offering reliable winds averaging 4 to 6 knots from April to October with 50 percent south winds. The area is suitable for beginners, intermediates, and experts, with numerous surf schools offering training and equipment rentals. Additional locations include Ilıca, Pırlanta, Akkum Teos in Seferihisar, Urla, Dikili, Foça, and Özbek, though the region is better known for windsurfing than traditional wave surfing.

name: Dubai
description: Ultra-modern city where natural surf meets artificial innovation. Sunset Beach near Burj Al Arab offers a standard beach break with sandy bottom working best from low to high tide, while North Beach at Pearl Jumeirah is a man-made break created by island construction that acts as the region's swell magnet, typically receiving bigger waves with multiple peaks and occasional barrels. Since Dubai isn't swell-rich, surfers often turn to the Wadi wave pool about an hour outside the city, offering consistent artificial waves designed by Kelly Slater comparable to the Kelly Slater Surf Ranch. The city's natural breaks are popular for surf lessons with dedicated surfing zones supervised by lifeguards year-round.

name: Umm al Qaywayn
description: Quiet emirate better known for kitesurfing than traditional wave surfing, with the Kite Beach Centre serving as the main water sports destination. The area offers a rustic beach setting described as the UAE's best-kept secret, though natural ocean surfing breaks are not well documented within the emirate itself. For serious surfing, visitors typically travel to nearby emirates with better-established breaks. The region maintains a pet-friendly atmosphere and focuses more on wind-powered water sports than swell-dependent surfing.

name: Da Nang
description: Central Vietnamese city with a surf scene that emerged from war-era roots, where China Beach gained fame among US soldiers during the Vietnam War. My Khe Beach serves as the most popular beginner spot with soft breaks and gentle consistent waves, while Non Nuoc Beach near the Marble Mountains offers longer waves for intermediate surfers practicing turns. Nam O Beach in the north provides powerful waves for experienced surfers with stunning views. The region has a short surf season with winds and swells active only about four months per year, with autumn particularly October offering the best conditions when clean waves occur about 48 percent of the time.

name: The Great Binh Thuận
description: Home to Mui Ne, Vietnam's premier surfing destination offering fairly consistent waves and shallow waters ideal for learning. The region features Little Buddha, a decent left-hand point break that works best on larger swells, and Main Beach with mushy beach breaks better suited for smaller days and beginners. Best conditions occur from October through February, with October being optimal though only 8 percent of the time are conditions clean while 43 percent are blown out. East swell combines with west-northwest offshore winds for best results, though by 11 a.m. kite winds typically destroy the surface. January through March see chest-to-head high punchy waves before wind picks up by late morning. The area is also popular for kitesurfing due to consistent wind conditions.

name: The Great Khánh Hòa
description: Province featuring Nha Trang and Bai Dai Beach, ranked third for surfing in Vietnam with soft sand and gentle waves suitable for beginners. The area offers numerous surf schools providing lessons and board rentals, making it accessible for those new to the sport. Best surfing conditions occur from November to March during the dry season when wind conditions are favorable and wave consistency is highest. The region provides a more relaxed alternative to Vietnam's premier surf destinations, with conditions that favor learning and progression over challenging advanced surfing.

name: Time Quang Nam
description: Central province sharing surf breaks with neighboring Da Nang, including spots like Lang Co and Cue Dai Beach. The region experiences the same short surf season with winds and swells typically active only about four months per year, with winter generally being the optimum time for consistent conditions. The area offers a mix of beach breaks and reef breaks with varying wave consistency, though surf infrastructure is less developed compared to the more established Da Nang scene to the immediate south.

name: The Great Thiên-Huế
description: Northern central province featuring Lang Co, which offers poor to fair conditions with 1 to 2 foot waves. The region is less developed for surfing compared to Vietnam's more established destinations further south, with limited surf infrastructure and inconsistent conditions. The area experiences the northeast monsoon season from November to March when most Vietnamese breaks fire, though wave quality and consistency remain lower than premier destinations like Mui Ne and Da Nang.

name: Flanders
description: Northern Belgian region with North Sea coastline featuring beach breaks at Westende, Middelkerke, and Oostende. Conditions range from inconsistent to fairly consistent, with most breaks offering both left and right-hand waves over sandy bottoms. Best swell direction comes from the north-northwest with ideal offshore winds from the southeast, though winter particularly January provides the best chance for clean waves. Even during optimal periods, surfable waves are rare with most days considered too small for most surfers. The region's breaks are rarely crowded even when conditions are favorable, with hazards including rips and obstacles at various locations.

name: Burgas
description: Southern Black Sea coastal area offering access to breaks including Lozenets Beach, Harmani, Melnitza, and Silistar. The region experiences autumn and winter as the best surfing periods when conditions are most favorable, though the Black Sea generally provides inconsistent summer weather and low winter temperatures. Sea temperatures average around 3 degrees Celsius in winter months, making a wetsuit essential for any surf session. The area serves as a gateway to Bulgaria's Black Sea surf scene with nearby airport access.

name: Dobrich Oblast
description: Northern Black Sea region home to Shabla, one of Bulgaria's best surf spots featuring good conditions at all stages of the tide with offshore southwestern winds. The area maintains relatively few surfers even on good days, offering a quieter alternative to more crowded destinations. The easternmost point of Bulgaria near the Romanian border, Shabla provides a sense of freedom and connection with nature along a 3.2-kilometer beach covered with clean golden sand mixed with seashells. The northern Black Sea coast features shorter summers and cooler waters than southern Bulgarian beaches, but offers less crowded conditions and beautiful natural settings.

name: Varna
description: Major Black Sea port city with access to breaks including Varvara and nearby spots along Bulgaria's northern coastline. The area experiences autumn and winter as optimal surfing periods when east swells arrive, though conditions can be inconsistent throughout the year. The Black Sea provides relatively few established surf breaks compared to ocean destinations, with sea temperatures dropping to around 3 degrees Celsius in winter requiring proper wetsuit protection. The city serves as a transportation hub for accessing Bulgaria's Black Sea surf scene.

name: Chlorakas
description: Coastal area near Paphos with access to Cyprus's western surf breaks. The region benefits from proximity to established spots like Governor's Beach and Paramali Beach, though the area itself has limited documented surf infrastructure. Winter provides peak season for consistent swells and favorable water conditions along Cyprus's Mediterranean coast, with mild temperatures around 20 degrees Celsius making it more comfortable than colder European destinations.

name: Episcopies
description: Limassol area location with surf reporting services tracking wave conditions, tides, and wind patterns. The region is part of Cyprus's broader surfing scene, which offers excellent winter surfing conditions with consistent swells and mild temperatures around 20 degrees Celsius. The area benefits from the island's Mediterranean location, providing diverse wave types suitable for different skill levels, though surf infrastructure remains less developed compared to more established European destinations.

name: Plakoton's Catalyst
description: Also known as Katalymata ton Plakoton, this surf reporting location near Limassol and the Pafos region is part of Cyprus's emerging surf scene. The area benefits from the island's winter swell patterns when consistent Mediterranean swells arrive, though the spot maintains limited documentation compared to more established breaks. The region offers access to Cyprus's diverse wave types while maintaining a quieter atmosphere away from the main tourist centers.

name: Larnaca
description: Eastern coastal city featuring Queen Victoria Beach, a beach break best surfed during winter with south-southeast swells and north-northwest offshore winds. The area offers several kitesurfing and watersports spots including McKenzie, Sailing Club, and Pervolia, though traditional wave surfing infrastructure is less developed than the western coast around Paphos. The region benefits from Cyprus's mild winter temperatures around 20 degrees Celsius, making it more comfortable than colder European surf destinations.

name: Paphos
description: Western coastal city serving as Cyprus's primary surf hub with established breaks including Packyammos, Governor's Beach, and Paramali Beach. Packyammos, also known as The Rock, is an advanced-only spot east of Pomos featuring double-head waves with right-hand breaking points and barreling sections, best for experienced surfers due to rough conditions and surrounding rocks. Governor's Beach offers both left and right-hand breaks on reefs and deep water suitable for intermediate surfers, while Paramali Beach provides beginner-friendly conditions with calm, mellow waves on a long sandy beach. Winter is peak season for consistent swells along this Mediterranean coastline.

name: Potima
description: Established surf break with dedicated forecast pages and surf reports, listed among Cyprus's best surf spots. Potima Point offers access to the island's excellent winter surfing conditions with consistent swells and mild temperatures around 20 degrees Celsius. The area benefits from Cyprus's Mediterranean location, providing diverse wave types suitable for different skill levels, though the spot maintains less documentation than more established breaks on the western coast around Paphos.

name: Capital Region
description: Copenhagen area with limited wave potential in the Kattegat, requiring very strong winds to generate rideable surf. The region's primary surf interest lies along the Baltic Sea coast around Stevns Klint and Rødvig, where waves break on moderate to strong easterly winds. Stevns Klint breaks on moderate E or SE winds, while Rødvig requires stronger E winds sustained for at least 8 hours. The area relies heavily on wind swell rather than groundswell, with conditions being fickle and often accompanied by onshore winds. For consistent surfing, locals typically travel to the North Sea coast on Jutland's west side.

name: Central Jutland
description: Jutland's central section with access to Denmark's primary North Sea surf region. The area includes breaks along the best stretch running from Klitmøller to Agger, with popular spots like Nr. Vorupør in between. The region features sand-bottomed breaks and rock reefs that clean up wind swell, with Klitmøller, also known as Cold Hawaii, offering reef breaks including Inside Reef and Bunkers known for quality right-hand waves. Best conditions occur September to April, with peak months in autumn when low-pressure systems stall near Iceland, though swells are fickle and often accompanied by onshore winds.

name: North Denmark
description: Northernmost district offering Denmark's best overall surf conditions along the North Sea coast. The region features Klitmøller, also known as Cold Hawaii, with reef breaks including Inside Reef and Bunkers known for quality right-hand waves. Hirtshals serves as the northernmost location with unpredictable banks due to dredging, while the area experiences heavy westerly winds year-round that can generate head-high waves within 12 hours. Winter water temperatures near 4 degrees Celsius require 6/4mm wetsuits, while summer allows 3/2mm suits in 17 degree Celsius water. The coast relies primarily on short-fetch wind swell rather than groundswells.

name: South Denmark
description: Southern region encompassing the Danish South Sea islands including Ærø, Lyø, and Langeland, emerging as new windsurfing and watersports destinations with picturesque bays and flatwater spots. The area is less established for traditional wave surfing compared to the North Sea coast on Jutland's west side, though the islands offer unique conditions in the protected waters south of Funen. The region maintains a quieter atmosphere away from Denmark's primary surf hubs, with conditions more suited to wind-powered water sports than swell-dependent surfing.

name: Zealand
description: Island region including Copenhagen with limited wave potential in the Kattegat, though the Baltic Sea coast offers wind-dependent surfing around Stevns Klint and Rødvig on the east coast. Stevns Klint breaks on moderate E or SE winds, while Rødvig requires stronger E winds sustained for at least 8 hours. The island of Bornholm in the Baltic reportedly gets as good as the North Sea coast, with numerous points and bays facing different directions. The region relies heavily on wind swell rather than groundswell, with conditions being fickle and often requiring very strong winds to generate rideable surf in the Kattegat near Copenhagen.

name: Bournemouth, Christchurch and Poole Council
description: The Boscombe artificial reef, built in the late 2000s in front of Boscombe Pier, was an ambitious attempt to create reliable south coast surf. While it never delivers perfectly consistent waves, it remains the go-to spot in the heart of the town, frequently surfed when winter south west swells push deep into the Channel. When swell angle and tide align, Boscombe and the surrounding Bournemouth beach can produce short, punchy peaks shaped by groynes and shifting sand. Long flat spells are common, and even the better days are brief, making local knowledge and timing far more important than any forecast.

name: Brighton
description: When a compact winter swell squeezes into the Channel, beaches around the marina and the piers can throw quick lefts and rights. It is nothing world class, but December and midwinter storms can give the best chance of a rideable window. Expect groynes, rips and the occasional water quality issue, plus crowds when it finally turns on.

name: Cornwall
description: The heart of UK surfing and where Atlantic swell hits first in England. Newquay’s Fistral is probably the most recognisable wave in the country, drawing surfers year‑round for small runners up to big swells that can activate the Cribbar’s big wave potential. Further south, Porthleven sits more exposed to open Atlantic swell, producing steeper, heavier waves that don’t always appear on the north coast. Cornwall offers more surfable days than anywhere else in England, but that reliability brings pressure, especially in summer when surf schools, visitors and party tourism crowd many lineups. Summer tends to be softer and busy, while autumn and winter bring bigger swell, stronger winds, and a more serious scene in the water.

name: Cumbria
description: Surf on the Cumbrian coast relies on Irish Sea weather systems and careful timing. When wind and tide cooperate, spots around Walney Island and Earnse Bay can produce workable beachbreak peaks, while more sheltered options suit smaller swells. Autumn and winter are the most reliable seasons, with large tides shaping conditions throughout the day. Consistency is limited, but patience is often rewarded.

name: Devon
description: Long sandy bays and headlands make this one of the most dependable surf corners of the south west. Croyde is the marquee beachbreak with punchier winter walls and easier summer surf, and Woolacombe offers a huge stretch that can handle a lot of swell. Putsborough and Saunton give options when wind and crowd change, and the North Devon World Surfing Reserve label reflects how central surfing is here.

name: Dorset
description: Patience is part of the deal on this stretch of the Jurassic Coast. Kimmeridge can deliver a proper right on bigger swells, while Swanage and Lyme Bay are more about short windows and tide sensitive peaks. Most of the time the surf is small or inconsistent, with the better days tied to the right Channel swell angle.

name: Durham
description: Cold water, shifting sand and a small local crew define the sessions here. Seaton Carew is the best known option, with beachbreak peaks that need a north or north east swell and a clean wind. The season leans hard toward late autumn through spring, when storms provide the push and the winds occasionally behave.

name: East Sussex
description: Channel swell is often modest here, so reading the tides and wind matters as much as the forecast. Camber Sands can offer playful sandbank peaks, and Birling Gap is a more exposed option when the swell has enough push. Eastbourne and Bexhill are generally fickle, but winter brings the most regular run of surfable days.

name: East Yorkshire
description: Most of the year it is small, but a clean northerly can make Bridlington worth the drive. The setup is simple beachbreak with groynes and rips, and the best sessions tend to happen around lower water when the banks line up. It is more of a beginner and improver zone than a guaranteed wave magnet.

name: Essex
description: Think short period wind swell over a shallow seabed, with brief windows when the wind swings offshore. Walton on the Naze is the best known name, and nearby stretches around Jaywick and Clacton can produce rideable peaks on the right north facing weather. Outside the warmer months, it can work weekly, but size is usually limited.

name: Hampshire
description: For Hampshire surfers, the conversation usually comes back to Hayling Island when the charts show a rare south coast pulse. Sandbars near the harbour mouths can shape fun, soft walls that suit longboards, with the occasional storm swell giving shortboard moments. Strong tides and shore dumpy water at high tide are part of the learning curve.

name: Isle of Wight
description: Having more than one coastline makes it easier to hunt for a surfable corner. Compton Bay is the headline beachbreak, with other options like Freshwater and Sandown offering different levels of shelter and crowd. The island has decades of surf history, but it still depends on the right Channel swell and wind to feel truly on.

name: Kent
description: Chalk, sandbars and a surprising number of bays give this corner more variety than most people expect. Joss Bay remains the historic hub, Viking Bay leans toward slower longboard lines, and Ramsgate can get punchier along the pier on the right swell. A north or north east push in winter is the best recipe, with tides playing a big role.

name: Lancashire
description: Big tides and a shallow Irish Sea shelf keep the waves mellow, but there are workable beachbreak days for those who watch the wind. St Annes and the Blackpool stretch are the usual starting points, with peaks that come and go with the banks. Autumn and winter bring the most consistent swell, even if it is rarely powerful.

name: Lincolnshire
description: A north wind over the North Sea can stack up short waves on this long sandy coast. Mablethorpe and Skegness are the typical check spots, with groynes and shifting banks deciding whether it is surfable or flat. Winter is the best bet, but consistency is never the selling point.

name: Norfolk
description: A mix of sandbars and small reefs keeps the sessions varied when the North Sea has enough energy. Cromer and East Runton are the familiar names, Sea Palling can break over its reef, and Mundesley and Gorleston fill in the gaps. The best run is October to March on northerly swell, often with cold water and silty looking surf.

name: North Tyneside
description: If there is swell in the North Sea, Longsands is usually where the crowd goes first. Tynemouth, Cullercoats and nearby bays offer a mix of sand and rock, with the cleanest days coming with a north or north east swell and a west or south west wind. It can be busy, but it is also one of the most reliable surf scenes on the east coast.

name: North Yorkshire
description: Cayton Bay is the workhorse here, handling anything from small beginner waves to solid overhead days depending on the swell. Different sections light up across the tide, with faster waves at lower water and mellower lines when it fills in, plus options around Scarborough when wind direction changes. Late autumn through spring is prime time for size and consistency.

name: Northumberland
description: Long beaches, cold water and fewer bodies in the lineup are the main appeal. Bamburgh, Druridge and other open stretches pick up north and north east swells well, with the best days tied to clean winds and solid winter storms. It is a place for patient surfers who do not mind driving between peaks.

name: Redcar and Cleveland
description: Saltburn is the headline when the banks are right, offering one of the cleaner setups on this section of the North Sea. Coatham Sands and nearby beaches can also produce rideable peaks, but it is mostly a winter game with plenty of wind dependence. When it clicks, the wave quality can surprise for such an industrial backdrop.

name: Suffolk
description: Small, clean runners and longboard sessions are the usual reward on this low profile coast. Walberswick and the Southwold area pick up northerly swells best, with groynes and shifting sand deciding where the peaks appear. Expect more short lived windswell than long period groundswell, with winter providing the most chances.

name: Sunderland
description: Between headlands and small bays, you can often find a corner to surf even when it looks flat from the road. Seaburn and Roker are the regular checks, with Whitburn and Marsden offering different angles when the swell has enough push. Late autumn through spring is the season, with north and north east swells doing most of the work.

name: Tyne and Wear
description: A quick check from the city can pay off when a northerly swell meets a west wind and the beaches line up. From the Tynemouth side down toward South Shields, the coast offers multiple sand and rock setups that suit different tides. It is not remote or empty, but it is one of the most consistent places to get wet on the east coast.

name: West Sussex
description: Sheltered geography means this stretch needs the right storm track to get moving. West Wittering is a common target thanks to its sandbars, and the coast around Worthing and Littlehampton can show rideable peaks on the better winter pulses. Most sessions are about making the most of smaller swells and clean winds.

name: Wirral
description: This peninsula is more about opportunistic wind swell than guaranteed surf. New Brighton on the Wallasey side is the usual check, but the tides are huge and currents can be strong near the Mersey. Many days are better for wind sports, so timing and local knowledge matter when you are chasing an actual wave.

name: Harjumaa
description: Close to Tallinn but still reliant on the right wind angle, this coast is mainly about short period Baltic wind swell. Stroomi, Kakumäe and Vääna Jõesuu are the usual checks, with better chances during late autumn and midwinter gales. Expect small windows, cold water, and a lot of days that are more about watching charts than surfing.

name: Hiiumaa
description: A windier, more wave friendly corner of the Baltic, with Ristna as the island’s best known break when storms line up. The surf is short period and tide is not the story, so it comes down to wind direction and enough fetch across open water. It feels remote, gets chilly fast, and rewards anyone willing to suit up for a rare clean session.

name: Faroe Islands
description: Cold water and deep Atlantic weather make the sessions here feel serious even when the waves are not huge. Tjørnuvík is the headline, a dramatic black sand bay that can handle north northwest swell with offshore winds, but conditions shift quickly with currents and wind. Crowds are minimal, and the main challenge is timing a clean window between squalls.

name: Central Ostrobothnia Region
description: Finland’s west coast is mostly wind driven and fickle, with waves that show up fast and vanish just as quickly. Around Kokkola and Tankar, the sea is short period and often shaped by strong winds, so it is more of a forecast watching game than a guaranteed surf mission. When it is on, the surf tends to suit fun boards and quick reactions.

name: Ostrobothnia Region
description: This coastline is more commonly associated with wind sports than steady surf, but strong systems can still kick up rideable wind swell. Vaasa sits on a shallow, low energy section of the Gulf of Bothnia, so most sessions are small and short lived. Think opportunistic beach peaks when the wind cooperates, not a regular swell pipeline.

name: Satakunta
description: Yyteri near Pori is the reference point here, a long sandy beach where Finnish surf culture took root. The surf is wind generated, so the best sessions come with the right storm track and cleanable winds rather than long period swell. It is a classic beachbreak setup with shifting banks, and it can feel surprisingly legit when the weather finally lines up.

name: Southwest Finland
description: With thousands of islands and sheltered water, this region is not built for consistent swell. When the Baltic does produce a workable push, you are usually hunting for an exposed corner and a wind that is not too onshore. Most days lean toward flatwater, so any surf here tends to be small, local, and very dependent on timing.

name: Uusimaa
description: Southern Finland’s most surf checked coastline, mostly because it actually has a few named options when the Baltic turns on. Hanko and Tulliniemi are the classic call on wind swell, and the wider Helsinki coast gets occasional short period peaks when the wind and fetch line up. Sessions are usually small and fast, with cold water and quick changes in conditions.

name: Brittany
description: Open Atlantic headlands give this coast one of the widest swell windows in mainland France. Beach peaks and reef points around La Torche, Crozon and the Quiberon peninsula light up on west to northwest swells, with autumn and winter bringing the most size. Expect strong tides, fast shifting sandbanks, and wind as the main quality limiter.

name: Corsica
description: An island set in open water, with reef and sand breaks that can turn on fast when a storm lines up. The west coast around Capo di Feno and other bays near Ajaccio catches winter wind swell, often linked to strong regional winds, and can get punchy on shallow rock. Swell windows can be short, so timing and wind direction matter as much as size.

name: Hauts-de-France
description: Along the Channel and North Sea fringe, surf is mostly short period wind swell with plenty of tidal movement. Beach breaks near Wissant and Wimereux can offer clean peaks when winds ease or turn offshore, but conditions change quickly with tide and sand. Expect smaller faces than the Atlantic and lots of days where wind is the whole story.

name: Normandy
description: Big tidal ranges shape most sessions here, turning the same stretch of sand into very different waves through a tide. On the Cotentin coast, spots around Siouville Hague are the usual magnets when Atlantic swells wrap in and the wind behaves. Autumn and winter bring the best chance of power, with rips and shifting banks as the main hazards.

name: Nouvelle-Aquitaine
description: Long open beaches and sandbars make this coastline the engine room of French surfing. Around Hossegor and Seignosse, west and northwest swells can produce heavy, hollow sandbar waves like La Gravière that change with banks and tide. Farther south, Biarritz and Anglet add reef and point setups that handle different angles and often stay more structured in wind. Autumn is the classic window when swell returns and easterly winds show up.

name: Occitanie
description: Most waves here come from brief Mediterranean lows, so swell periods are shorter and windows are tighter than the Atlantic. Beach breaks around Leucate, La Franqui and Gruissan can deliver punchy peaks when east or southeast swell meets a clean wind angle. Expect lots of small days, then quick sessions when a storm passes and the sand lines up.

name: Pays de la Loire
description: Sand and rock mix along this stretch, creating everything from soft beach peaks to serious ledges. Vendée is the focal point, with the La Sauzaie reef and the Bud Bud beachbreak standing out when west swell and light winds hit together. Autumn and winter are most consistent, and the tide can make or break a spot in a couple of hours.

name: Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur
description: Surf on this Mediterranean coast is opportunistic, relying on south swells and the occasional clean wind window. Around Marseille, spots like Carro, La Couronne and Prado can break when a storm sends enough push and the Mistral drops or turns favorable. Waves are usually short period and inconsistent, with reefs and jetties offering the best shape when it lines up.

name: Reunion
description: In the Indian Ocean, coral reef passes and points can turn heavy very quickly. The Saint Leu area and nearby breaks such as Trois Bassins are known for long walls on south swell, with the main season running roughly April to September. Shallow reef, strong currents and local shark management rules are part of the reality here, so conditions and access can be as important as the forecast.

name: Ajaria
description: Black Sea coast around Batumi with modest windswell and small beachbreaks. Stony shores and resort strips from Khelvachauri to Kobuleti; surf reports exist but waves are typically small and conditions fickle.

name: Gamogma-Kariata
description: Inland area in Khobi Municipality with no coastline; included for geographic coverage rather than surf.

name: kuchkisabano
description: Coastal strip near Ureki and Shekvetili known for magnetic-sand beaches and resort development. Occasional small windswell on the Black Sea; kitesurf and beach tourism more common than wave surfing.

name: New Grigoleti
description: Seaside village in Guria with distinctive magnetic sand and a low-key resort feel. Black Sea windswell is possible but rarely documented; better known for sand therapy and calm waters.

name: Samegrelo-Zemo Svaneti
description: Northwestern region taking in both Black Sea shoreline and high mountains. Kolkheti National Park borders the coast; surf is scarce and wind-dependent, with the area better known for kayaking and nature.

name: Lower Saxony
description: North Sea coast and East Frisian Islands such as Norderney and Borkum. NNW groundswells and a large tidal range; timing and wind windows are key for short, often choppy sessions.

name: Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania
description: Baltic coast with wave and kite spots from Warnemünde to Rügen. The ferry-induced standing wave at Warnemünde is a novelty; elsewhere, westerly and north-easterly winds drive beachbreaks and sideshore conditions.

name: Schleswig-Holstein
description: Home to Sylt and St. Peter-Ording on the North Sea, plus Baltic exposure. Strong tidal range and SW to W windswells; best on mid to high tide when offshore banks get enough water.

name: Munich
description: Inland city famous for the Eisbach standing wave in the Englischer Garten, a river break surfable year-round. Experienced surfers only; the wave is powerful and the spot is tightly regulated. Be friendly and respectful and you my be able to give it a go.

name: Catalan Bay
description: Eastern beach on the Rock with Mediterranean swell from the Strait; best from autumn to spring. Caleta and Sandy Bay offer barreling shore break and bodyboard-friendly peaks. Surf restricted 10am to 8pm in summer.

name: Attica
description: Athens and the surrounding coast with spots such as Vouliagmeni, Rafina, and Sounio. Conditions are often flat; winter westerlies occasionally bring rideable windswell to exposed beaches.

name: Central Macedonia
description: Thessaloniki and the Thermaic Gulf; limited open coast. Surf is scarce and not a primary destination; most activity is windsurf or kitesurf on sheltered waters.

name: Crete
description: Island far enough south to escape much of Greece’s swell shadow. Chania and Falasarna see the best winter westerlies; quality is inconsistent but among the best in the country.

name: Epirus
description: Premier surf region in Greece with the highest density of breaks. Beachbreaks, reefs, and rivermouths from Kastro to Loutsa; southern Ionian and Adriatic swells, best in winter.

name: Ionian Islands
description: Western island chain catching longer-period swells than most of Greece. Corfu’s Agios Giorgios and Glyfada lead the pack; Lefkada and others see windswell when westerlies blow.

name: North Aegean
description: Islands such as Lesbos, Samos, and Ikaria where summer Meltemi builds small windswell. Tinos and Kolymbithra offer some of the better summer waves in Greece, though size stays modest.

name: Peloponnese
description: Western coast around Messinia holds some of Greece’s prettiest waves in winter. Lagouvardos is a standout beach break; Costa Navarino and nearby spots can get busy when swell hits.

name: South Aegean
description: Cyclades and Dodecanese with north-facing coasts that pick up Meltemi windswell in summer. Rhodes and Prasonisi get additional east and south swell; Naxos, Mykonos, and Kos see smaller, fickle waves.

name: Thessaly
description: Central mainland with a short stretch of coast on the Pelion peninsula. Surf is limited and inconsistent; the region is better known for mountains and culture than for waves.

name: West Greece
description: Ionian-facing coast from Patras to the Peloponnese border. Some exposure to westerly swells; conditions are less consistent than Epirus or the Ionian Islands but can produce waves in winter.

name: Castel
description: Northwest parish taking in Vazon and Cobo, the island’s main surf coast. Vazon is the primary beginner beach with a surf school and novice area; Cobo and Port Soif add sandy options. Tides strongly affect when breaks work.

name: La Picaterre
description: Small coastal locality on Guernsey with typical Channel Island exposure. Surf is limited and conditions depend on swell direction and tide; the island’s main breaks lie in Castel and L’Ancresse.

name: L'Ancresse
description: Northern bay and one of Guernsey’s best beach breaks when conditions align. Works best on the drop from high tide; hollow peaks and better right-handers. Inconsistent, mostly winter; Pembroke Bay sits alongside.

name: Saint Anne
description: Main settlement on Alderney, part of the Bailiwick. The island has several beaches and some exposure to Atlantic swells; surf is sporadic and less documented than on Guernsey.

name: Saint Peter Port
description: Capital and main port on the east coast. Havelet Bay and nearby spots can pick up swell; tides and wind matter. Reef breaks suit experienced surfers; beginners are directed to Vazon in Castel.

name: St. Andrew's
description: Central parish with no coastline; the only landlocked parish on Guernsey. No direct surf; access to the coast is via neighbouring parishes such as Castel and St. Saviour.

name: St. Sampson
description: Northern parish around the harbour and Braye du Valle. Some north-facing shore; surf is secondary to the port. Best waves remain in Castel and L’Ancresse.

name: St. Saviour
description: West-coast parish with inland and coastal fringes. Closer to the main surf exposure than St. Andrew’s; conditions still depend on swell and tide, with Vazon and L’Ancresse the main spots.

name: Capital Region
description: Reykjavík and its surrounds hold surf right in the capital, including Grotta at the western tip, so you can base in the city and still get waves before heading south or onto the peninsula. Cold water and full wetsuits year-round; hot springs, harbour life, and the chance of northern lights give the region its character.

name: East
description: Largely undocumented surf along the east coast: ash-grey beachbreaks in empty bays, with very little traffic. The draw is empty lineups and raw coastline; behind them, Breiðdalur’s rhyolite peaks, reindeer, and salmon rivers.

name: Northeast
description: Scarce, exploratory surf on the north coast; the region is better known for Lake Mývatn’s geothermal baths, Dettifoss, and the Diamond Circle. Whale watching and midnight sun in summer.

name: South
description: Heavy, hollow beachbreaks at Vik, with swell funnelled down a submarine canyon; rips and cold water make it serious. Inland: glacier tongues, Jökulsárlón, Diamond Beach, and waterfalls like Seljalandsfoss and Skógafoss. The south is Iceland at its most dramatic, in and out of the water.

name: Southern Peninsula
description: Iceland’s main surf hub: a long, powerful point that put the country on the map, now under pressure from harbour development. Atlantic swells, black sand, reef and beach breaks; autumn and winter deliver the best conditions. Volcanic Reykjanes: lava fields, the Blue Lagoon, and the rift where two continental plates pull apart.

name: West
description: Peeling rights and remote beachbreaks, often with 4WD access; less crowded than Reykjanes, same North Atlantic swell and cold water. Snæfellsjökull’s glacier-capped volcano, lava caves, and cliffs where puffins and arctic terns nest give it a wilder, more isolated feel.

name: Connaught
description: Home to Mullaghmore, Ireland’s premier big-wave slab; Strandhill and Enniscrone offer beachbreaks and a right-hand reef. Full Atlantic exposure, emerald coast, cold water; best in autumn and winter.

name: Leinster
description: East coast picks up less Atlantic swell; Wicklow’s Brittas Bay and Bray are mainly beachbreaks with easy Dublin access. Quieter and more fickle, with the same cold water and green coastline.

name: Munster
description: Kerry’s Dingle Peninsula and Inch Reefs, Cork and Clare: inconsistent but scenic. Inch Reef a long, rarely-on right; Brandon Bay, beachbreaks and reefs. Wild Atlantic Way; surf schools and camps from spring to autumn.

name: Ulster
description: Donegal Bay is Ireland’s surf epicentre: triangulated reefs, rivermouth sandbars and beachbreaks. Bundoran’s The Peak reef and Tullan Strand beachbreak; Easkey’s limestone reefs. Competition venue, emerald coast; best in autumn and winter.

name: Isle of Man
description: Irish Sea island with Peel as the main surf hub. Beach and reef breaks on the west coast, big tidal range and cold water. Best on SW swell in autumn and winter. TT racing heritage and quiet coast when the crowds are elsewhere.

name: Abruzzo
description: Adriatic coast south of the Marche with Pescara and Vasto. Rare SE and NE swells, mostly windswell. Sandy beaches and trabocchi coast. Few surfable days but uncrowded when it works.

name: Apulia
description: Heel of Italy facing the Adriatic and Ionian. Long coast, rare SE and S swells. Gargano and Salento get the most action. White towns, olive groves and empty lineups when swell hits.

name: Calabria
description: Toe of the boot with Tyrrhenian and Ionian exposure. Rare W and SW swells on the west, S on the east. Scilla and the Costa Viola when conditions align. Dramatic coast, few surfers.

name: Campania
description: Naples and the Amalfi coast. Tyrrhenian Sea, rare W and SW swells. A few beachbreaks and points when the Med delivers. Crowds in summer, better in autumn. Pizza, lemons and volcanic backdrop.

name: Emilia-Romagna
description: Ravenna and Rimini coast. Adriatic windswell and rare SE storms. Most consistent slice of Italian coast for surf. Beachbreaks and rivermouths. Crowded on good days. Piadina and beach culture.

name: Friuli Venezia Giulia
description: North-east corner of Italy, Gulf of Trieste. Adriatic windswell and rare NE storms. Lignano and Grado get the odd day. Cold in winter, quiet lineups.

name: Latium
description: Rome and the Lazio coast. Tyrrhenian Sea, rare W and SW swells. A few spots when the Med delivers. Ostia and Sabaudia. Ancient capital, beach culture and pasta.

name: Liguria
description: Italian Riviera from Genoa to the French border. NW Mistral and rare W swells. Levante and Ponente, a few beachbreaks when it lines up. Cinque Terre and focaccia.

name: Sardinia
description: Swell from every direction finds Sardinia, which holds the largest and most consistent waves in the Med. Reefs and points on the west coast take the dominant NW Mistral. Putzu Idu and the Capos are the focus. West coast beautifully untouched, nuraghi and shepherd culture inland.

name: Sicily
description: NW swell hits the north coast and Palermo. South and east rely on rarer SE wind and African lows, maybe 50 days a year, but when it lines up you get some of the Med's sweetest left points. Volcanoes, Greek temples and street food.

name: The Marches
description: Ancona and the coast north to Pesaro. Adriatic windswell and rare SE storms. Senigallia and the Riviera del Conero when it lines up. Hills and medieval towns inland.

name: Tuscany
description: Forte dei Marmi, Viareggio and Livorno are swell and people magnets when the Mistral builds. Beachbreaks often first to show the swell, Livorno's reefs good in the post-front glass-off. Elba offers hollow reefs in rare SE to SW swells. Historic heart of Italian surfing, fish markets and Tuscan wine.

name: Veneto
description: Sixty to a hundred surfable days a year on the shallow Adriatic, many of them weak NE windswell. Ravenna and the coast attract crowds on rare SE swells and NE storms. Less consistent than Emilia-Romagna. Venice and the Lido when the rare swell hits. La Serenissima, cicchetti and spritz after.

name: Gorey
description: East coast harbour under Mont Orgueil, sheltered from the main Atlantic swell. Picks up easterly windswell and can offer an option when the west coast is blown out or oversized, with the same huge tidal range governing when and where it works.

name: Le Hocq
description: South coast headland that receives windswell from the south and east. A local spot that works on the right tide and wind, with the island's big tidal range making timing essential.

name: Les Laveurs
description: Sits inside St Ouen's Bay, the island's main swell magnet. Beach breaks and shifting banks with the Watersplash at the heart of the scene and some of Europe's last untapped big-wave reef potential when conditions align.

name: Saint Helier
description: South coast town where spots east of the harbour work on certain tides when Atlantic swell wraps or when local windswell builds. Tide knowledge is critical and the lineup can get busy when it turns on.

name: Saint Saviour
description: Parish wrapping the capital with access to town-side breaks and the bays either side. Tide timing governs everything and local knowledge pays when the swell and wind align.

name: Liepāja
description: Baltic city with exposure to westerly winds and short-period chop. Cold water and limited fetch mean windswell only, best in strong north-westerlies when Pavilosta and the coast come to life.

name: Pavilostas Novads
description: Home to Pavilosta, Latvia's best-known surf spot. Beach breaks and windswell when conditions align, with cold water and short fetch typical of the Baltic.

name: Riga
description: Capital on the Gulf of Riga with mostly flat water and occasional windswell reaching the coast near Bolderāja. Cold, short-fetch conditions when it does get waves. Popular with surfers based in the city when the Baltic delivers.

name: Ventspils Municipality
description: Baltic coastline with the same short-fetch, windswell-only picture as the rest of Latvia. Cold water and a small local scene when the wind and swell line up.

name: Klaipeda County
description: Lithuania's main coastline with small windswell and chop. Nida and the Curonian Spit get the most attention from kiters and occasional surfers in cold, marginal conditions.

name: Il-Mellieħa
description: North Malta with Mellieha Bay as the main named break, a sandbar that rarely gets proper swell but can work on east swell with offshore wind. Mediterranean fetch limits consistency.

name: Iż-Zebbuġ
description: North-west coast pocket with very limited surf and mostly windswell when it reaches the coast. Same Mediterranean constraints as the rest of the island.

name: Friesland
description: Wadden Sea coast and the Frisian islands: Vlieland, Terschelling, Ameland and Schiermonnikoog. North Sea side of the islands gets windswell and chop; tidal range is huge so timing matters. Quieter than the Holland provinces, same cold water and winter NW windswell.

name: North Holland
description: Zandvoort is the main draw, an exposed beach break that works best in spring when the North Sea lines up. More room than South Holland, same cold water and unreliable swell. Petten and Callantsoog north of the province.

name: Province of Zealand
description: Gets Channel and winter windswell first, so the most consistent Dutch coast. Domburg and Brouwersdam are the focus, with long beaches, groynes and shifting banks. Grevelingen and designated surf zones when it pumps.

name: South Holland
description: Scheveningen is the hub, with the country's largest surf festival and most surf schools. Hoek van Holland offers peeling lefts off the pier. Busy and learner-friendly when the North Sea delivers.

name: Ards and North Down
description: Eastern coast facing the Irish Sea, with Strangford Lough and the Ards Peninsula. Less exposed than the Causeway, but picks up swell when it wraps. Quieter lineups and cold water.

name: Causeway Coast and Glens
description: Portrush is the hub, with East Strand, West Strand and Whiterocks. Portstewart Strand, Benone and Castlerock along the same stretch. Atlantic swell, best in autumn and winter. The most surfed part of the Irish coast.

name: Co Down
description: Murlough under the Mourne Mountains is the main break, a long sandy beach that catches the wind and swell. Tyrella and Newcastle when it lines up. Strong winds, learner-friendly, quieter than the Causeway.

name: Agder
description: Southern Norway coast around Kristiansand. North Sea windswell, cold water and inconsistent swell. Few documented breaks compared to Jæren further west. Rocky and sandy coast when conditions line up.

name: Finnmark
description: Arctic coast from Nordkapp to the Russian border. Short-fetch Barents Sea windswell, cold water and rare clean days. Hamningberg and other exposed points get small waves when wind and swell align. For committed cold-water surfers only.

name: Møre og Romsdal Fylke
description: Stad Peninsula and Molde area with powerful NW swells over rock and sand. Hoddevik is the standout beach break and one of Norway’s best spots, decent on most NW swells. Winter brings bigger, colder surf; summer is mellower.

name: Nordland Fylke
description: Lofoten and the rest of the county above the Arctic Circle. Unstad is the famous left point with sand and stone, fed by Norwegian Sea swells. Cold water, winter peak season and midnight sun in summer for mellower waves.

name: Rogaland Fylke
description: Jæren coast near Stavanger with 70 km of beaches and direct North Sea exposure. Bore and Sele are the main breaks; sandy and pebble beach breaks on S, W and NW swells. Not the most consistent but surfable year-round with cold water in winter.

name: Sogn og Fjordane Fylke
description: Western fjords with Hoddevik as the main spot, a tight surf community and strong NW swells. Beginner to intermediate beach break, best in autumn and spring; summer is softer. Nordfjord surf and Scandinavia’s first wavepool at Sandane.

name: Telemark
description: Inland and Skagerrak coast in the south. Limited surf; a few spots pick up southerly swells when conditions align. Mostly known for lakes and mountains rather than consistent waves.

name: Troms and Finnmark
description: Merged northern county from Tromsø to the North Cape. Exposed Barents and Norwegian Sea coast with short-fetch windswell and rare clean days. Cold water and remote lineups for surfers seeking empty Arctic waves.

name: Vest-Agder Fylke
description: Southern tip of Norway around Kristiansand. North Sea windswell, cold water and fewer breaks than Jæren to the west. Rocky and sandy coast when swell and wind align.

name: Vestfold and Telemark
description: Southeast coast including Larvik and the Vestfold shore. Saltstein and a few spots work on southerly swells but are unreliable. Mostly sheltered; best when rare S swells push into the Skagerrak.

name: Vestland
description: Large county from Bergen north along the fjord coast. Hoddevik and Refviksanden lie north of Bergen; winter Atlantic swells, summer flat spells. Bore Surf Senter in Bergen; fjord scenery and cold water year-round.

name: Pomeranian
description: Baltic coast around the Tri-City and the Hel Peninsula. Short-fetch windswell, best when strong W or NW winds blow. Hel is known for wind and kitesurf; surfable beach breaks when swell lines up. Cold water and fickle conditions.

name: West Pomerania
description: Western Polish Baltic from the German border to Słupsk. Dąbki, Dźwirzyno and Międzyzdroje are the main spots; windswell when onshore W winds blow, spring and autumn storms best. Shallow lagoons for learning, beach breaks when the Baltic kicks up.

name: Región del Libertador General Bernardo O’Higgins
